kids.question: What angel appeared to Zacharias in the temple?
kids.option_a: Gabriel
kids.explanation: Luke 1:19 — "I am Gabriel, that stand in the presence of God; and am sent to speak unto thee, and to shew thee these glad tidings."

kids.question: What was the name of the baby that the angel said Mary would have?
kids.option_a: Jesus
kids.explanation: Luke 1:31 — "And, behold, thou shalt conceive in thy womb, and bring forth a son, and shalt call his name JESUS."

kids.question: What happened to Zacharias because he did not believe the angel?
kids.option_a: He could not speak until the baby was born
kids.explanation: Luke 1:20 — "And, behold, thou shalt be dumb, and not able to speak, until the day that these things shall be performed, because thou believest not my words."

teens.question: What did the angel Gabriel say to Mary about her favour with God?
teens.option_a: Hail, thou that art highly favoured, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women
teens.option_b: You are the richest woman in Israel
teens.option_c: You have been chosen as a queen
teens.option_d: You are wiser than all the prophets
teens.explanation: Luke 1:28 — "And the angel came in unto her, and said, Hail, thou that art highly favoured, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women."

teens.question: How did the angel explain that Mary would conceive while being a virgin?
teens.option_a: The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee
teens.option_b: By a special potion from the temple
teens.option_c: Through a visit from an earthly king
teens.option_d: By eating sacred bread
teens.explanation: Luke 1:35 — "The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee: therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God."

teens.question: What famous statement did the angel make about God's power?
teens.option_a: For with God nothing shall be impossible
teens.option_b: God only helps those who help themselves
teens.option_c: God works only through human effort
teens.option_d: God's power has limits on earth
teens.explanation: Luke 1:37 — "For with God nothing shall be impossible."

teens.question: What did Mary say when she began to praise God?
teens.option_a: My soul doth magnify the Lord, and my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Saviour
teens.option_b: I am the greatest woman in Israel
teens.option_c: I deserve this honour more than anyone
teens.option_d: I have earned this by my good works
teens.explanation: Luke 1:46-47 — "And Mary said, My soul doth magnify the Lord, And my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Saviour."

teens.question: What happened when Zacharias wrote "His name is John" on a writing table?
teens.option_a: His mouth was opened immediately and he spake, and praised God
teens.option_b: He fell into a deep sleep
teens.option_c: The writing table broke in half
teens.option_d: Nothing happened until the next day
teens.explanation: Luke 1:63-64 — "And he asked for a writing table, and wrote, saying, His name is John. And they marvelled all. And his mouth was opened immediately, and his tongue loosed, and he spake, and praised God."

adults.question: How does Luke describe the purpose of his Gospel in the opening verses?
adults.option_a: To write an orderly account so that Theophilus might know the certainty of those things wherein he was instructed
adults.option_b: To replace all previous accounts of Jesus' life
adults.option_c: To provide a legal defence for Paul
adults.option_d: To create a liturgical text for temple worship
adults.explanation: Luke 1:3-4 — "It seemed good to me also, having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first, to write unto thee in order, most excellent Theophilus, That thou mightest know the certainty of those things, wherein thou hast been instructed."

adults.question: What was Zacharias doing when the angel appeared to him?
adults.option_a: Burning incense in the temple of the Lord, his lot having been chosen by priestly custom
adults.option_b: Reading the Torah in the synagogue
adults.option_c: Baptising people in the Jordan
adults.option_d: Offering a lamb on the altar
adults.explanation: Luke 1:9-11 — "According to the custom of the priest's office, his lot was to burn incense when he went into the temple of the Lord. And there appeared unto him an angel of the Lord standing on the right side of the altar of incense."

adults.question: What does the angel say about John the Baptist's ministry in relation to the coming Messiah?
adults.option_a: He shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just
adults.option_b: He shall be a military leader who conquers Rome
adults.option_c: He shall become the high priest of Israel
adults.option_d: He shall write new scriptures for the nation
adults.explanation: Luke 1:17 — "And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord."

adults.question: What Old Testament prophecy does the angel's description of John fulfil?
adults.option_a: Malachi 4:5-6 — the promise of Elijah returning to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children before the great day of the Lord
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53 — the suffering servant
adults.option_c: Psalm 22 — the cry of the forsaken
adults.option_d: Daniel 9 — the seventy weeks prophecy
adults.explanation: Luke 1:17 echoes Malachi 4:5-6 — "Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD: And he shall turn the heart of the fathers to the children."

adults.question: In Mary's Magnificat, what does she say God has done to the proud?
adults.option_a: He hath scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts
adults.option_b: He has rewarded the proud for their confidence
adults.option_c: He has ignored the proud completely
adults.option_d: He has promoted the proud to positions of power
adults.explanation: Luke 1:51 — "He hath shewed strength with his arm; he hath scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts."

adults.question: What does Zacharias prophesy about the "horn of salvation" in the Benedictus?
adults.option_a: God hath raised up an horn of salvation in the house of His servant David — as spoken by the holy prophets since the world began
adults.option_b: A literal horn will appear in the temple
adults.option_c: David himself will return to reign
adults.option_d: A new military weapon will deliver Israel
adults.explanation: Luke 1:69-70 — "And hath raised up an horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David; As he spake by the mouth of his holy prophets, which have been since the world began."

adults.question: What does Zacharias say John will give to God's people?
adults.option_a: Knowledge of salvation by the remission of their sins, through the tender mercy of our God
adults.option_b: Political freedom from Roman rule
adults.option_c: Great material wealth and prosperity
adults.option_d: Military strategies for victory
adults.explanation: Luke 1:77-78 — "To give knowledge of salvation unto his people by the remission of their sins, Through the tender mercy of our God; whereby the dayspring from on high hath visited us."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the "dayspring from on high" in Zacharias' prophecy?
adults.option_a: It refers to the Messiah as the rising sun from heaven — to give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, and to guide our feet into the way of peace
adults.option_b: It describes a literal sunrise over the temple
adults.option_c: It refers to a new star appearing in the sky
adults.option_d: It describes the spring season arriving early
adults.explanation: Luke 1:78-79 — "The dayspring from on high hath visited us, To give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the way of peace." The Greek "anatole" means sunrise/dawn, a messianic title.

kids.question: Where was baby Jesus laid after He was born?
kids.option_a: In a manger
kids.option_d: In a basket on the river
kids.explanation: Luke 2:7 — "And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger; because there was no room for them in the inn."

kids.question: Who did the angels appear to with the good news about Jesus' birth?
kids.option_a: Shepherds keeping watch over their flock by night
kids.explanation: Luke 2:8-10 — "And there were in the same country shepherds abiding in the field, keeping watch over their flock by night. And, lo, the angel of the Lord came upon them."

kids.question: What did the angels sing when they told the shepherds about Jesus?
kids.option_a: Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men
kids.option_d: Praise the king of the Romans
kids.explanation: Luke 2:14 — "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men."

teens.question: Why did Joseph and Mary travel to Bethlehem?
teens.option_a: Caesar Augustus decreed that all the world should be taxed, and Joseph went to Bethlehem because he was of the house of David
teens.option_b: They were fleeing from King Herod
teens.option_c: They were visiting Mary's parents
teens.option_d: They wanted to celebrate Passover there
teens.explanation: Luke 2:1-4 — "And it came to pass in those days, that there went out a decree from Caesar Augustus, that all the world should be taxed... And Joseph also went up from Galilee... unto Bethlehem; because he was of the house and lineage of David."

teens.question: What did Simeon say when he held the baby Jesus in the temple?
teens.option_a: Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, for mine eyes have seen thy salvation
teens.option_b: This child will be a great warrior
teens.option_c: This child will become a wealthy merchant
teens.option_d: This child will rebuild the temple
teens.explanation: Luke 2:29-30 — "Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, according to thy word: For mine eyes have seen thy salvation."

teens.question: What prophecy did Simeon give Mary about Jesus?
teens.option_a: A sword shall pierce through thy own soul also — and this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel
teens.option_b: Your son will become the richest man in the world
teens.option_c: Your son will never face any hardship
teens.option_d: Your son will rule as an earthly king immediately
teens.explanation: Luke 2:34-35 — "Behold, this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel; and for a sign which shall be spoken against; (Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also.)"

teens.question: What did twelve-year-old Jesus say when His parents found Him in the temple?
teens.option_a: Wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?
teens.option_b: I was lost and could not find my way
teens.option_c: I was playing with the other children
teens.option_d: I was waiting for you to come back
teens.explanation: Luke 2:49 — "And he said unto them, How is it that ye sought me? wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?"

teens.question: How does Luke summarise Jesus' growth as a child?
teens.option_a: Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man
teens.option_b: Jesus showed no sign of being special as a child
teens.option_c: Jesus performed many miracles as a boy
teens.option_d: Jesus spent all His time alone in prayer
teens.explanation: Luke 2:52 — "And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man."

adults.question: What is the historical significance of Luke mentioning Caesar Augustus and the taxing decree?
adults.option_a: It anchors the birth of Christ in verifiable world history — Luke, the careful historian, ties the incarnation to a specific Roman census under Augustus
adults.option_b: It shows Jesus was born into a wealthy family
adults.option_c: It proves Rome supported Christianity
adults.option_d: It was included to flatter the Roman emperor
adults.explanation: Luke 2:1-2 — "There went out a decree from Caesar Augustus, that all the world should be taxed. (And this taxing was first made when Cyrenius was governor of Syria.)" Luke uniquely ties sacred history to secular chronology.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of God announcing Jesus' birth to shepherds rather than to royalty?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates God's pattern of revealing salvation to the humble and lowly — shepherds were among the lowest social classes, reflecting Luke's theme of gospel for the poor
adults.option_b: Shepherds were the wealthiest class in Israel
adults.option_c: There were no royalty nearby to tell
adults.option_d: It was simply a random coincidence
adults.explanation: Luke 2:8-12 — The announcement to shepherds fits Luke's theme of good news for the poor and marginalized (cf. Luke 4:18, 6:20). God bypasses the powerful to reveal His Son to the humble.

adults.question: What does the angel's title for Jesus — "a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord" — combine?
adults.option_a: Three distinct roles: Saviour (deliverer), Christ (Messiah/anointed one), and Lord (divine sovereign) — a uniquely comprehensive Christological declaration
adults.option_b: Only a political title for Jewish kings
adults.option_c: A common name used for Roman generals
adults.option_d: A title reserved for temple priests
adults.explanation: Luke 2:11 — "For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord." This triple title uniquely combines soteriology (Saviour), messianic identity (Christ), and deity (Lord/Kyrios).

adults.question: What does Simeon's Nunc Dimittis reveal about the scope of salvation?
adults.option_a: Salvation is prepared before the face of all people — a light to lighten the Gentiles, and the glory of God's people Israel
adults.option_b: Salvation is only for the Jewish nation
adults.option_c: Salvation is only for temple worshippers
adults.option_d: Salvation is limited to those in Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Luke 2:30-32 — "For mine eyes have seen thy salvation, Which thou hast prepared before the face of all people; A light to lighten the Gentiles, and the glory of thy people Israel." Simeon sees both universal and particular aspects of salvation.

adults.question: Who was the prophetess Anna, and what did she do upon seeing Jesus?
adults.option_a: A prophetess, daughter of Phanuel of the tribe of Aser, who served God with fastings and prayers night and day — she gave thanks and spake of him to all that looked for redemption in Jerusalem
adults.option_b: A Roman official's wife who ignored Jesus
adults.option_c: A wealthy merchant who offered gold
adults.option_d: A scribe who recorded the event officially
adults.explanation: Luke 2:36-38 — "And there was one Anna, a prophetess, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Aser... which departed not from the temple, but served God with fastings and prayers night and day. And she... gave thanks likewise unto the Lord, and spake of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem."

adults.question: What does Simeon's prophecy about a "sword" piercing Mary's soul foreshadow?
adults.option_a: A literal sword attack on Mary
adults.option_b: Mary's anguish at the crucifixion of her son — the revelation of Jesus as the suffering Messiah would bring her deep personal grief
adults.option_c: Mary would become a warrior
adults.option_d: Mary would leave Israel permanently
adults.explanation: Luke 2:35 — "(Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also.)" This prophetically anticipates Mary standing at the cross (John 19:25), experiencing the suffering inherent in her son's mission.

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement about being "about my Father's business" at age twelve reveal?
adults.option_a: Even as a child, Jesus had a conscious awareness of His unique divine sonship and His mission from the Father
adults.option_b: Jesus was disobedient to His earthly parents
adults.option_c: Jesus wanted to become a scribe
adults.option_d: Jesus was confused about His identity
adults.explanation: Luke 2:49 — "Wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?" This is Jesus' first recorded words, revealing His self-awareness of His unique relationship with God as Father and His divine mission.

adults.question: How does Luke 2:52 reflect the full humanity of Christ?
adults.option_a: By stating that Jesus increased in wisdom, stature, and favour with God and man — showing genuine human development in every dimension: intellectual, physical, spiritual, and social
adults.option_b: It suggests Jesus was not truly divine
adults.option_c: It implies Jesus had limitations He never overcame
adults.option_d: It shows Jesus was like any ordinary person with no special calling
adults.explanation: Luke 2:52 — "And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man." This verse affirms the orthodox doctrine that Jesus was fully human, experiencing real growth, while remaining fully divine.

kids.question: Who baptised people in the Jordan River and told them to repent?
kids.option_a: John the Baptist
kids.explanation: Luke 3:3 — "And he came into all the country about Jordan, preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins."

kids.question: What came down from heaven when Jesus was baptised?
kids.option_a: The Holy Ghost in the shape of a dove
kids.explanation: Luke 3:22 — "And the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily shape like a dove upon him, and a voice came from heaven, which said, Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased."

kids.question: What did the voice from heaven say when Jesus was baptised?
kids.option_a: Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased
kids.option_c: This is the king of Israel
kids.option_d: This man will rule the world
kids.explanation: Luke 3:22 — "And a voice came from heaven, which said, Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased."

teens.question: What Old Testament passage does Luke quote about John the Baptist?
teens.option_a: The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight — from Isaiah 40:3
teens.option_b: The Lord is my shepherd — from Psalm 23
teens.option_c: In the beginning God created — from Genesis 1
teens.option_d: Behold the Lamb of God — from Isaiah 53
teens.explanation: Luke 3:4 — "As it is written in the book of the words of Esaias the prophet, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight."

teens.question: What did John tell people who had two coats?
teens.option_a: He that hath two coats, let him impart to him that hath none; and he that hath meat, let him do likewise
teens.option_b: Keep both coats for yourself
teens.option_c: Sell both and give the money to the temple
teens.option_d: Give them both to the priests
teens.explanation: Luke 3:11 — "He that hath two coats, let him impart to him that hath none; and he that hath meat, let him do likewise."

teens.question: What did John say about the One coming after him?
teens.option_a: One mightier than I cometh, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire
teens.option_b: Someone who will be weaker than me
teens.option_c: A political leader who will overthrow Rome
teens.option_d: A priest who will rebuild the temple
teens.explanation: Luke 3:16 — "John answered, saying unto them all, I indeed baptize you with water; but one mightier than I cometh, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire."

teens.question: What did John tell the publicans (tax collectors) who came to be baptised?
teens.option_a: Exact no more than that which is appointed you — collect only what is required
teens.option_b: Stop collecting taxes entirely
teens.option_c: Give all the money to the poor
teens.option_d: Leave your job immediately
teens.explanation: Luke 3:13 — "And he said unto them, Exact no more than that which is appointed you."

teens.question: How far back does Luke trace Jesus' genealogy?
teens.option_a: All the way back to Adam, which was the son of God
teens.option_b: Back to Abraham only
teens.option_c: Back to David only
teens.option_d: Back to Moses only
teens.explanation: Luke 3:38 — "Which was the son of Enos, which was the son of Seth, which was the son of Adam, which was the son of God." Luke traces Jesus' line back to Adam, emphasizing Jesus as the Saviour of all humanity.

adults.question: Why does Luke provide such precise historical dating in verses 1-2?
adults.option_a: To establish the reliability of his account by anchoring John's ministry in verifiable history — naming Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate, Herod, Philip, Lysanias, and the high priests Annas and Caiaphas
adults.option_b: To impress wealthy Roman readers
adults.option_c: To show off his knowledge of politics
adults.option_d: To fill space in his manuscript
adults.explanation: Luke 3:1-2 — Luke names six political rulers and two high priests, providing a sixfold synchronism that precisely dates the beginning of John's ministry to approximately AD 28-29.

adults.question: What is significant about Luke extending the Isaiah 40 quotation further than Matthew and Mark?
adults.option_a: Luke alone includes "all flesh shall see the salvation of God" — emphasising the universal scope of salvation, consistent with his Gentile audience and theme of gospel for all peoples
adults.option_b: Luke made an error in his quotation
adults.option_c: Luke was quoting a different passage
adults.option_d: There is no difference between the Gospel accounts
adults.explanation: Luke 3:6 — "And all flesh shall see the salvation of God." Matthew and Mark stop earlier, but Luke extends to Isaiah 40:5, highlighting universality — a key Lukan theme.

adults.question: What does John mean by "fruits worthy of repentance"?
adults.option_a: Genuine repentance must produce visible evidence in changed behaviour — not mere words or religious heritage, as shown by his specific ethical instructions to crowds, publicans, and soldiers
adults.option_b: Literal fruit must be brought as an offering
adults.option_c: Repentance is only an internal feeling
adults.option_d: Only priests need to show repentance
adults.explanation: Luke 3:8 — "Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and begin not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father." John demands concrete ethical change, not reliance on Abrahamic descent.

adults.question: What does the image of the "fan in his hand" and "unquenchable fire" signify about the coming Messiah?
adults.option_a: The Messiah will be a judge who separates the righteous from the wicked — the winnowing fan separates wheat from chaff, and the unquenchable fire represents final judgment
adults.option_b: Jesus will be a farmer
adults.option_c: Jesus will bring literal fire to earth
adults.option_d: John was confused about Jesus' role
adults.explanation: Luke 3:17 — "Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable." This is an agricultural metaphor for eschatological judgment.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of all three Persons of the Trinity being present at Jesus' baptism?
adults.option_a: It is a Trinitarian theophany — the Son is baptised, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father speaks from heaven, revealing the triune nature of God in one event
adults.option_b: It was merely a coincidence
adults.option_c: Only one person of God was present
adults.option_d: The Trinity doctrine was invented later
adults.explanation: Luke 3:21-22 — Jesus (Son) is praying, the Holy Ghost descends in bodily shape like a dove, and the Father's voice declares from heaven. This is one of the clearest Trinitarian revelations in the Gospels.

adults.question: Why does Luke's genealogy differ from Matthew's, and what is its theological purpose?
adults.option_a: Luke likely traces Jesus' line through Mary (Heli being Mary's father), going back to Adam to show Jesus as Saviour of all humanity — whereas Matthew traces through Joseph to David and Abraham for a Jewish audience
adults.option_b: Luke made errors in his genealogy
adults.option_c: The two genealogies are completely contradictory
adults.option_d: Luke copied Matthew and changed names
adults.explanation: Luke 3:23-38 — Luke's genealogy ascends from Jesus to Adam ("son of God"), emphasising universal humanity. Matthew descends from Abraham to Jesus, emphasising Jewish kingship. The traditional view is that Luke traces Mary's line.

adults.question: What does John's rebuke "who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come" imply about his audience?
adults.option_a: John was surprised anyone came at all
adults.option_b: Everyone who came was genuinely repentant
adults.option_c: John was refusing to baptise anyone
adults.option_d: Some came for baptism as a religious formality without genuine repentance — John challenged their motives and demanded authentic heart change
adults.explanation: Luke 3:7 — "Then said he to the multitude that came forth to be baptized of him, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?" John discerns superficial religion and demands genuine transformation.

adults.question: Why was Herod's imprisonment of John significant for Luke's narrative?
adults.option_a: Luke mentions it before the baptism account to close John's ministry section before opening Jesus' public ministry — showing the transition from the old prophetic era to the new messianic age
adults.option_b: Luke made a chronological error
adults.option_c: It was unimportant to the narrative
adults.option_d: Luke wanted to criticise Roman politics
adults.explanation: Luke 3:19-20 — By mentioning Herod's imprisonment of John before narrating Jesus' baptism (v.21), Luke literarily closes the Baptist's ministry, marking the decisive transition from preparation to fulfilment.

kids.question: Who tempted Jesus in the wilderness?
kids.option_a: The devil
kids.explanation: Luke 4:2 — "Being forty days tempted of the devil."

kids.question: How long did Jesus fast in the wilderness?
kids.option_a: Forty days
kids.explanation: Luke 4:2 — "Being forty days tempted of the devil. And in those days he did eat nothing."

kids.question: What happened when Jesus read from the scroll in the synagogue at Nazareth?
kids.option_a: The people were angry and tried to push Him off a cliff
kids.option_b: The people cheered and crowned Him king
kids.explanation: Luke 4:28-29 — "And all they in the synagogue, when they heard these things, were filled with wrath, And rose up, and thrust him out of the city, and led him unto the brow of the hill... that they might cast him down headlong."

teens.question: What did Jesus answer when the devil tempted Him to turn stones into bread?
teens.option_a: It is written, That man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God
teens.option_b: I am too tired to use my power
teens.option_c: I will eat after you leave
teens.option_d: Bread is not important to me
teens.explanation: Luke 4:4 — "And Jesus answered him, saying, It is written, That man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God."

teens.question: What passage from Isaiah did Jesus read in the Nazareth synagogue?
teens.option_a: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor
teens.option_b: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth
teens.option_c: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
teens.option_d: Thou shalt have no other gods before me
teens.explanation: Luke 4:18 — "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor; he hath sent me to heal the brokenhearted, to preach deliverance to the captives, and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty them that are bruised."

teens.question: What did Jesus declare after reading the Isaiah scroll?
teens.option_a: This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears
teens.option_b: This scripture is about a future prophet
teens.option_c: This passage has already been fulfilled by Elijah
teens.option_d: This does not apply to anyone alive today
teens.explanation: Luke 4:21 — "And he began to say unto them, This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears."

teens.question: What proverb did Jesus say the people of Nazareth would use against Him?
teens.option_a: Physician, heal thyself — and No prophet is accepted in his own country
teens.option_b: A rolling stone gathers no moss
teens.option_c: Pride goeth before a fall
teens.option_d: The early bird catches the worm
teens.explanation: Luke 4:23-24 — "Ye will surely say unto me this proverb, Physician, heal thyself... Verily I say unto you, No prophet is accepted in his own country."

teens.question: What examples did Jesus use of God sending prophets to Gentiles instead of Israelites?
teens.option_a: Elijah was sent to a widow in Sidon, and Elisha healed Naaman the Syrian — both were Gentiles
teens.option_b: Moses was sent to Egypt, and Joshua to Jericho
teens.option_c: David fled to Moab, and Solomon traded with Tyre
teens.option_d: Jonah went to Nineveh, and Daniel to Babylon
teens.explanation: Luke 4:25-27 — Jesus cites the widow of Sarepta (Sidon) who hosted Elijah (1 Kings 17) and Naaman the Syrian cleansed by Elisha (2 Kings 5), showing God's grace extends to Gentiles.

adults.question: How does Luke's order of the three temptations differ from Matthew's, and what is the significance?
adults.option_a: Luke places the temple temptation last (climaxing in Jerusalem), while Matthew places the mountain temptation last — Luke's order reflects his theological emphasis on Jerusalem as the destination of Jesus' ministry
adults.option_b: There is no difference between the accounts
adults.option_c: Luke omits one of the temptations
adults.option_d: Matthew added an extra temptation
adults.explanation: Luke 4:9-12 — Luke's third temptation is at the Jerusalem temple, while Matthew ends with the mountain vision. Luke's Jerusalem-centric structure anticipates his travel narrative (9:51) where Jesus resolutely heads to Jerusalem.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus stopping mid-sentence when reading Isaiah 61 in the synagogue?
adults.option_a: Jesus read "the acceptable year of the Lord" but stopped before "the day of vengeance of our God" — indicating that His first coming brings salvation and grace, with judgment reserved for the second coming
adults.option_b: Jesus lost his place in the scroll
adults.option_c: The scroll was damaged at that point
adults.option_d: Jesus was interrupted by the congregation
adults.explanation: Luke 4:19 — Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1-2a but stops before 61:2b ("the day of vengeance of our God"). This deliberate omission distinguishes the first advent (grace) from the second (judgment).

adults.question: What does Jesus' use of the Elijah and Elisha examples reveal about His mission?
adults.option_a: God's saving work extends beyond Israel to Gentiles — Jesus provocatively reminds Nazareth that God bypassed many Israelites to bless Gentile outsiders, foreshadowing the Gentile mission
adults.option_b: Jesus was simply teaching Old Testament history
adults.option_c: Jesus was praising Nazareth's faithfulness
adults.option_d: Jesus wanted to start a political revolution
adults.explanation: Luke 4:25-27 — By citing the Sidonian widow and Naaman, Jesus implies that unbelieving Israel may be passed over in favour of believing Gentiles — a theme central to Luke-Acts.

adults.question: What does the phrase "he passing through the midst of them went his way" suggest?
adults.option_a: A supernatural escape — Jesus walked through the hostile crowd unharmed, suggesting divine protection because His hour had not yet come
adults.option_b: Jesus ran away quickly
adults.option_c: The crowd simply lost interest
adults.option_d: Jesus disguised himself and escaped
adults.explanation: Luke 4:30 — "But he passing through the midst of them went his way." The calm, sovereign manner of escape implies miraculous deliverance, echoing John 7:30 and 8:59.

adults.question: What did the demons in the synagogue at Capernaum recognise about Jesus?
adults.option_a: They knew He was "the Holy One of God" and "Christ" — the demons possessed supernatural knowledge of Jesus' true identity before many humans recognised it
adults.option_b: They thought He was just a prophet
adults.option_c: They did not recognise Him at all
adults.option_d: They believed He was John the Baptist
adults.explanation: Luke 4:34,41 — "I know thee who thou art; the Holy One of God" (v.34) and "Thou art Christ the Son of God" (v.41). Ironically, demonic testimony precedes human confession.

adults.question: Why did Jesus command the demons to be silent about His identity?
adults.option_a: Jesus did not want His messianic identity proclaimed by demons — the "messianic secret" prevented premature disclosure that could lead to misunderstanding of His mission as political rather than redemptive
adults.option_b: Jesus was not sure of His own identity
adults.option_c: The demons were lying about who He was
adults.option_d: Jesus wanted to keep a low profile for safety
adults.explanation: Luke 4:35,41 — "He rebuking them suffered them not to speak: for they knew that he was Christ." Jesus controls the timing and manner of His self-revelation, refusing demonic testimony.

adults.question: What does Jesus' healing of Simon's wife's mother demonstrate about His power?
adults.option_a: Jesus rebuked the fever and it left her immediately, and she arose and ministered unto them — showing authority over disease comparable to His authority over demons
adults.option_b: Jesus used medicine to heal her
adults.option_c: The fever went away on its own
adults.option_d: Peter's prayer healed her, not Jesus
adults.explanation: Luke 4:39 — "And he stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her: and immediately she arose and ministered unto them." Luke uniquely uses "rebuked" (the same word used for demons), showing Jesus' sovereign authority over all affliction.

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement "I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent" reveal about His priorities?
adults.option_a: Preaching the kingdom of God was the primary purpose of Jesus' mission — healing was a sign, but proclamation of the kingdom was His central calling
adults.option_b: Jesus was tired of healing and wanted to do something else
adults.option_c: Jesus preferred larger cities over small towns
adults.option_d: Jesus was running from His enemies
adults.explanation: Luke 4:43 — "I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent." Jesus defines His mission as proclamation, not merely miraculous works, preventing a mere wonder-worker reputation.

kids.question: What miracle happened when Jesus told Simon Peter to let down his nets?
kids.option_a: They caught so many fish that the nets began to break
kids.option_c: The fish jumped onto the boat
kids.explanation: Luke 5:6 — "And when they had this done, they inclosed a great multitude of fishes: and their net brake."

kids.question: How did some friends get a sick man to Jesus when the house was too crowded?
kids.option_a: They lowered him through the roof on his bed
kids.option_b: They pushed through the front door
kids.option_c: They waited outside until everyone left
kids.option_d: They called Jesus to come outside
kids.explanation: Luke 5:19 — "And when they could not find by what way they might bring him in because of the multitude, they went upon the housetop, and let him down through the tiling with his couch into the midst before Jesus."

kids.question: What did Jesus say to the paralysed man?
kids.option_a: Man, thy sins are forgiven thee
kids.explanation: Luke 5:20 — "And when he saw their faith, he said unto him, Man, thy sins are forgiven thee."

teens.question: What did Peter say to Jesus after seeing the miraculous catch of fish?
teens.option_a: Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord
teens.option_b: Thank you for all these fish
teens.option_c: Let us sell these fish for a great price
teens.option_d: I knew you could do this all along
teens.explanation: Luke 5:8 — "When Simon Peter saw it, he fell down at Jesus' knees, saying, Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord."

teens.question: What did Jesus promise Simon Peter after the miraculous catch?
teens.option_a: From henceforth thou shalt catch men — Jesus called him to become a fisher of men
teens.option_b: You will become the richest fisherman in Galilee
teens.option_c: You will never have to fish again
teens.option_d: You will own all the boats on the sea
teens.explanation: Luke 5:10 — "And Jesus said unto Simon, Fear not; from henceforth thou shalt catch men."

teens.question: Why were the Pharisees upset when Jesus forgave the paralysed man's sins?
teens.option_a: They believed only God can forgive sins, so Jesus was claiming to be God — which they considered blasphemy
teens.option_b: They were upset the man was healed
teens.option_c: They thought Jesus should charge money
teens.option_d: They wanted to forgive the man themselves
teens.explanation: Luke 5:21 — "And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, Who is this which speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?"

teens.question: What tax collector did Jesus call to follow Him?
teens.option_a: Levi (also called Matthew), who was sitting at the receipt of custom
teens.option_b: Zacchaeus from Jericho
teens.option_c: Nicodemus the Pharisee
teens.option_d: Joseph of Arimathaea
teens.explanation: Luke 5:27 — "And after these things he went forth, and saw a publican, named Levi, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he said unto him, Follow me."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about who He came to call?
teens.option_a: They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick — I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance
teens.option_b: I came to call the most religious people
teens.option_c: I came to call the wealthiest citizens
teens.option_d: I came to call the strongest warriors
teens.explanation: Luke 5:31-32 — "They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Peter's response "Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord"?
adults.option_a: It echoes Old Testament theophanies (Isaiah 6:5) — Peter's awareness of his sinfulness in the presence of divine holiness shows he recognises Jesus as more than human
adults.option_b: Peter was simply being modest
adults.option_c: Peter was afraid of drowning
adults.option_d: Peter wanted Jesus to leave so he could sell the fish
adults.explanation: Luke 5:8 — Peter's reaction mirrors Isaiah's "Woe is me! for I am undone" (Isaiah 6:5). The miraculous catch reveals Jesus' divine authority, provoking the proper human response to holiness: conviction of sin.

adults.question: How does the healing of the leper demonstrate both Jesus' compassion and His respect for the Mosaic law?
adults.option_a: Jesus touched the leper (showing compassion to the untouchable) but then told him to show himself to the priest and offer the gift Moses commanded — honouring the law while transcending its boundaries
adults.option_b: Jesus avoided the leper completely
adults.option_c: Jesus told the leper to ignore the law
adults.option_d: Jesus healed him from a distance and gave no further instructions
adults.explanation: Luke 5:13-14 — "And he put forth his hand, and touched him... and immediately the leprosy departed... go, and shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing, according as Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them."

adults.question: What does Jesus' question "Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?" prove about His authority?
adults.option_a: The visible miracle (healing) validates the invisible one (forgiveness) — since both require divine power, the observable healing proves Jesus has the unseen authority to forgive sins
adults.option_b: Healing is easier than forgiving sins
adults.option_c: Forgiving sins is easier than healing
adults.option_d: Neither requires any real power
adults.explanation: Luke 5:23-24 — Jesus uses the irrefutable visible miracle to authenticate the contested invisible claim. If He can do the harder-to-verify act (forgiveness), He can certainly do the easier-to-verify one (healing).

adults.question: What is the significance of the title "Son of man" that Jesus uses in verse 24?
adults.option_a: It is Jesus' preferred self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7:13-14, combining humanity with divine authority — "the Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins"
adults.option_b: It simply means Jesus is a human being
adults.option_c: It is a title of weakness and humility only
adults.option_d: It was a common nickname in first-century Judaism
adults.explanation: Luke 5:24 — "That ye may know that the Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins." The title from Daniel 7:13-14 denotes one who receives everlasting dominion from the Ancient of Days — a claim to divine authority.

adults.question: What does Levi's great feast for Jesus reveal about his response to the call?
adults.option_a: Levi immediately left everything, followed Jesus, and then used his resources and social connections to introduce other tax collectors and sinners to Jesus — evangelism through hospitality
adults.option_b: Levi was simply showing off his wealth
adults.option_c: Levi was trying to bribe Jesus
adults.option_d: Levi wanted to prove he was still wealthy
adults.explanation: Luke 5:28-29 — "And he left all, rose up, and followed him. And Levi made him a great feast in his own house: and there was a great company of publicans and of others that sat down with them." Levi's first act as a disciple is evangelistic outreach.

adults.question: What does the parable of the new wine in old bottles (wineskins) teach in context?
adults.option_a: Jesus' teaching and ministry represent something radically new that cannot be contained within the rigid forms of Pharisaic Judaism — new wine needs new wineskins
adults.option_b: Wine should always be stored in old bottles
adults.option_c: The Pharisees' traditions were better than Jesus' teaching
adults.option_d: There is no real difference between old and new
adults.explanation: Luke 5:37-38 — "And no man putteth new wine into old bottles; else the new wine will burst the bottles... But new wine must be put into new bottles." The gospel of grace cannot be confined to legalistic structures.

adults.question: What does the phrase "they forsook all, and followed him" (v.11) tell us about the cost of discipleship?
adults.option_a: They brought their fishing business with them
adults.option_b: They only followed Jesus part-time
adults.option_c: True discipleship requires total abandonment of former securities — Peter, James, and John left their nets, boats, and livelihood at the peak of success (a miraculous catch) to follow Jesus
adults.option_d: They planned to return to fishing later
adults.explanation: Luke 5:11 — "And when they had brought their ships to land, they forsook all, and followed him." The greatest catch of their careers became the moment they walked away from fishing forever.

adults.question: What does verse 39 — "No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better" — mean in context?
adults.option_a: It acknowledges the difficulty of accepting new revelation when attached to familiar traditions — people naturally prefer what they are accustomed to, explaining why the religious establishment resisted Jesus
adults.option_b: Old wine is always better than new wine
adults.option_c: Jesus was recommending aged wine
adults.option_d: The Pharisees had better theology than Jesus
adults.explanation: Luke 5:39 — This proverb explains why the Pharisees and John's disciples struggled with Jesus' new approach. Human nature clings to the familiar. Jesus acknowledges this tendency without endorsing the old over the new.

kids.question: What did Jesus say is the Golden Rule?
kids.option_a: As ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise — treat others the way you want to be treated
kids.option_c: Go to bed early every night
kids.explanation: Luke 6:31 — "And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise."

kids.question: What did Jesus say we should do to our enemies?
kids.option_a: Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you
kids.option_d: Tell on them to someone bigger
kids.explanation: Luke 6:27 — "But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you."

kids.question: What happens to the house that is built without a good foundation?
kids.option_a: It falls down and is completely ruined
kids.option_c: It floats away on the water
kids.explanation: Luke 6:49 — "But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about judging others?
teens.option_a: Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven
teens.option_b: Always judge others so you can correct them
teens.option_c: Judging is only for the Pharisees
teens.option_d: Never form any opinion about anyone
teens.explanation: Luke 6:37 — "Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about giving?
teens.option_a: Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over
teens.option_b: Give only to people who can pay you back
teens.option_c: Never give anything away
teens.option_d: Give only when forced to
teens.explanation: Luke 6:38 — "Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom."

teens.question: What illustration did Jesus use about a speck and a beam?
teens.option_a: Do not try to remove the mote (speck) from your brother's eye when you have a beam (log) in your own eye — deal with your own faults first
teens.option_b: Always help others before yourself
teens.option_c: Only carpenters should handle beams
teens.option_d: Eye problems should be treated by doctors only
teens.explanation: Luke 6:41-42 — "And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?"

teens.question: What did Jesus say about a tree and its fruit?
teens.option_a: Every good tree bringeth forth good fruit — you can tell what a person is like by what they produce
teens.option_b: Bad trees sometimes produce good fruit
teens.option_c: You cannot tell anything about a tree from its fruit
teens.option_d: All trees produce the same fruit
teens.explanation: Luke 6:43-44 — "For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit. For every tree is known by his own fruit."

teens.question: What challenge did Jesus give to those who call Him "Lord"?
teens.option_a: Why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say? — obedience must match confession
teens.option_b: As long as you call Him Lord, your actions do not matter
teens.option_c: Only say Lord once, not twice
teens.option_d: Calling Him Lord is all you need to do
teens.explanation: Luke 6:46 — "And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?"

adults.question: What did Jesus mean by declaring Himself "Lord also of the sabbath"?
adults.option_a: As the Son of Man, Jesus has divine authority over the Sabbath — He who instituted it has the right to define its proper use, placing His authority above Pharisaic tradition
adults.option_b: Jesus was abolishing the Sabbath entirely
adults.option_c: Jesus was just another rabbi with an opinion
adults.option_d: The Sabbath was no longer important
adults.explanation: Luke 6:5 — "And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath." This claim to lordship over a divine institution is an implicit claim to deity.

adults.question: How does Luke's Sermon on the Plain (6:20-49) compare with Matthew's Sermon on the Mount?
adults.option_a: Luke's version is shorter, uses second person ("Blessed be ye poor"), includes woes alongside blessings, and emphasises socioeconomic themes — reflecting Luke's concern for the poor and marginalized
adults.option_b: They are identical in every detail
adults.option_c: Luke's version is longer than Matthew's
adults.option_d: Luke copied Matthew word for word
adults.explanation: Luke 6:20 — "Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God." Unlike Matthew's "poor in spirit," Luke's direct address to the literally poor fits his theme of reversal (1:52-53). The parallel woes (6:24-26) strengthen the contrast.

adults.question: What is the significance of the four "woes" in Luke 6:24-26?
adults.option_a: They reverse the four beatitudes — warning the rich, full, laughing, and well-spoken-of that present comfort may indicate spiritual danger, echoing the prophetic tradition of pronouncing judgment
adults.option_b: Jesus was condemning all wealthy people
adults.option_c: The woes were added by a later editor
adults.option_d: They only applied to Romans
adults.explanation: Luke 6:24-26 — "But woe unto you that are rich!... Woe unto you that are full!... Woe unto you that laugh now!... Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you!" These woes, unique to Luke, parallel OT prophetic oracles.

adults.question: What radical ethic does Jesus teach about loving enemies that goes beyond the Old Testament?
adults.option_a: Love, do good, bless, and pray for those who hate, curse, despitefully use, and persecute you — a fourfold response of grace that transcends the lex talionis and natural human reciprocity
adults.option_b: Simply avoid your enemies
adults.option_c: Only love enemies who repent first
adults.option_d: Love enemies in theory but not in practice
adults.explanation: Luke 6:27-28 — "Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you, Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you." Jesus commands active benevolence, not merely passive tolerance.

adults.question: What does Jesus' command to "lend, hoping for nothing again" teach about Christian generosity?
adults.option_a: Believers should lend without expecting repayment — true generosity is not a transaction but reflects the character of God, who "is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil"
adults.option_b: Christians should never lend money
adults.option_c: Only lend to people who can repay
adults.option_d: Lending must always include interest
adults.explanation: Luke 6:35 — "But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil."

adults.question: What does "Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful" establish as the standard for ethics?
adults.option_a: God's own character is the standard — believers are to imitate divine mercy, not merely follow rules, making theomorphic ethics (God-likeness) the basis of conduct
adults.option_b: Human consensus determines morality
adults.option_c: Cultural norms define right and wrong
adults.option_d: Personal feelings are the ultimate guide
adults.explanation: Luke 6:36 — "Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful." The parallel in Matthew 5:48 says "perfect." Luke's emphasis on mercy fits his Gospel's compassion theme.

adults.question: What does the parable of the blind leading the blind teach about spiritual leadership?
adults.option_a: An unqualified teacher will lead followers into the same ditch — spiritual leaders must first see clearly themselves before guiding others, as the disciple is not above his master
adults.option_b: All leaders are equally qualified
adults.option_c: Blindness is irrelevant to teaching
adults.option_d: Students should teach their teachers
adults.explanation: Luke 6:39-40 — "Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch? The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master."

adults.question: What is the parable of the two builders teaching about the relationship between hearing and doing?
adults.option_a: True faith requires both hearing and obeying Jesus' words — the one who hears and does builds on rock and withstands storms, while the one who hears but does not obey builds without foundation and faces ruin
adults.option_b: Simply hearing Jesus' words is sufficient
adults.option_c: Building a physical house guarantees salvation
adults.option_d: The parable is about construction techniques only
adults.explanation: Luke 6:47-49 — "Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them... is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock." The test of discipleship is obedience, not mere knowledge.

kids.question: What Roman soldier asked Jesus to heal his servant?
kids.option_a: A centurion — a Roman officer with great faith
kids.option_b: A general who was Jesus' friend
kids.option_c: A soldier who did not believe in God
kids.explanation: Luke 7:2-3 — "And a certain centurion's servant, who was dear unto him, was sick, and ready to die. And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant."

kids.question: What happened when Jesus met a funeral procession in the town of Nain?
kids.option_a: He raised the widow's dead son back to life
kids.option_c: He told the people not to cry
kids.option_d: He went to a different town
kids.explanation: Luke 7:14-15 — "And he came and touched the bier: and they that bare him stood still. And he said, Young man, I say unto thee, Arise. And he that was dead sat up, and began to speak."

kids.question: What did a woman do to show her love for Jesus at a Pharisee's house?
kids.option_a: She washed His feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair
kids.option_b: She cooked Him a big dinner
kids.option_c: She gave Him a golden crown
kids.option_d: She sang a song for Him
kids.explanation: Luke 7:38 — "And stood at his feet behind him weeping, and began to wash his feet with tears, and did wipe them with the hairs of her head, and kissed his feet, and anointed them with the ointment."

teens.question: Why was Jesus amazed at the centurion?
teens.option_a: He had not found so great faith, no, not in Israel — the centurion believed Jesus could heal with just a word
teens.option_b: The centurion was very wealthy
teens.option_c: The centurion had built a synagogue
teens.option_d: The centurion knew many languages
teens.explanation: Luke 7:9 — "When Jesus heard these things, he marvelled at him, and turned him about, and said unto the people that followed him, I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel."

teens.question: What question did John the Baptist send his disciples to ask Jesus from prison?
teens.option_a: Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?
teens.option_b: When will you free me from prison?
teens.option_c: Why have you forgotten about me?
teens.option_d: Will you become the king of Israel now?
teens.explanation: Luke 7:19 — "And John calling unto him two of his disciples sent them to Jesus, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?"

teens.question: How did Jesus answer John's question about whether He was the Messiah?
teens.option_a: He pointed to His works — the blind see, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor hear the gospel
teens.option_b: He said yes with a simple one-word answer
teens.option_c: He refused to answer the question
teens.option_d: He sent John a written letter
teens.explanation: Luke 7:22 — "Go your way, and tell John what things ye have seen and heard; how that the blind see, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, to the poor the gospel is preached."

teens.question: What parable did Jesus tell Simon the Pharisee about two debtors?
teens.option_a: One owed 500 pence and the other 50; when both were forgiven, the one forgiven more loved the creditor more — illustrating why the sinful woman loved Jesus so greatly
teens.option_b: One debtor paid and the other did not
teens.option_c: Both debtors refused to repay
teens.option_d: The creditor punished both equally
teens.explanation: Luke 7:41-43 — "There was a certain creditor which had two debtors: the one owed five hundred pence, and the other fifty. And when they had nothing to pay, he frankly forgave them both. Tell me therefore, which of them will love him most?"

teens.question: What did Jesus say to the sinful woman who anointed His feet?
teens.option_a: Thy sins are forgiven... Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace
teens.option_b: You must first prove you are worthy
teens.option_c: Go and sin no more before I forgive you
teens.option_d: I cannot forgive you here
teens.explanation: Luke 7:48,50 — "And he said unto her, Thy sins are forgiven... And he said to the woman, Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace."

adults.question: What does the centurion's reasoning about authority reveal about his understanding of Jesus?
adults.option_a: He understood that just as he gave commands under Roman authority and was obeyed, Jesus could command sickness with divine authority and be obeyed — recognising Jesus' supernatural jurisdiction over disease
adults.option_b: He thought Jesus needed to be physically present
adults.option_c: He believed healing required a ritual
adults.option_d: He thought Jesus was merely a good doctor
adults.explanation: Luke 7:7-8 — "But say in a word, and my servant shall be healed. For I also am a man set under authority, having under me soldiers, and I say unto one, Go, and he goeth." The centurion's military analogy shows he grasped Jesus' delegated divine authority.

adults.question: How does the raising of the widow's son at Nain echo Old Testament miracles?
adults.option_a: It directly parallels Elijah raising the widow's son at Zarephath (1 Kings 17:17-24) — Luke intentionally draws this parallel, as the crowd responds: "A great prophet is risen up among us; God hath visited his people"
adults.option_b: There is no Old Testament parallel
adults.option_c: It mirrors Moses parting the Red Sea
adults.option_d: It resembles David defeating Goliath
adults.explanation: Luke 7:15-16 — "And he delivered him to his mother" echoes 1 Kings 17:23 ("and delivered him unto his mother"). The crowd's response — "a great prophet" — shows they recognise the Elijah typology.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus' answer to John the Baptist in relation to Isaiah's prophecies?
adults.option_a: The answer had no connection to Old Testament prophecy
adults.option_b: Jesus was simply listing His daily activities
adults.option_c: Jesus' reply directly cites Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 — miracles of healing and gospel preaching that Isaiah prophesied the Messiah would perform, giving John evidence to confirm Jesus' identity from Scripture
adults.option_d: Jesus was avoiding the question
adults.explanation: Luke 7:22 — Jesus'  answer combines Isaiah 35:5-6 (blind see, lame walk, deaf hear) with Isaiah 61:1 (gospel to the poor), inviting John to see messianic prophecy fulfilled before his disciples' eyes.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "Blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me"?
adults.option_a: Those who do not stumble over Jesus' unexpected methods — His messiahship does not match political expectations, and blessed are those who accept God's plan even when it differs from their own
adults.option_b: No one has ever been offended by Jesus
adults.option_c: Jesus was warning people to be polite
adults.option_d: Jesus was predicting that no one would reject Him
adults.explanation: Luke 7:23 — "And blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me." The Greek "skandalizo" means to stumble. John expected a judging Messiah (3:17) but Jesus came in mercy. The blessing is on those who accept this.

adults.question: What does Jesus say about John the Baptist's role in the history of redemption?
adults.option_a: Among those born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist — yet the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he, because the kingdom era surpasses the prophetic era
adults.option_b: John was an ordinary prophet
adults.option_c: John was less important than Moses
adults.option_d: John was greater than Jesus
adults.explanation: Luke 7:28 — "For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist: but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he." John was the greatest of the old order; the least in the new kingdom surpasses him positionally.

adults.question: What does the contrast between Simon the Pharisee and the sinful woman teach about the relationship between forgiveness and love?
adults.option_a: The one who is forgiven much loves much — the woman's extravagant devotion flowed from her awareness of being greatly forgiven, while Simon's coldness reflected his failure to recognise his own need for forgiveness
adults.option_b: Simon was more righteous than the woman
adults.option_c: The woman was trying to earn forgiveness by her actions
adults.option_d: Love must come before forgiveness
adults.explanation: Luke 7:47 — "Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little." The love is the evidence of forgiveness already received, not the cause of it.

adults.question: What does the dinner guests' question "Who is this that forgiveth sins also?" reveal?
adults.option_a: They correctly understood that forgiving sins is a divine prerogative — their question is Christological, recognising that Jesus' claim to forgive implies a claim to deity
adults.option_b: They were asking a trivial social question
adults.option_c: They already knew the answer
adults.option_d: They were trying to be polite
adults.explanation: Luke 7:49 — "And they that sat at meat with him began to say within themselves, Who is this that forgiveth sins also?" Their reaction echoes 5:21 and shows that Jesus' forgiveness claims raised the question of His identity repeatedly.

adults.question: How does Jesus' characterisation of "this generation" as children in the marketplace illustrate Israel's rejection?
adults.option_a: They are like children who refuse to play whether the music is happy or sad — they rejected John for his asceticism and Jesus for His sociability, showing that the real issue was their unwillingness to repent, not the messenger's style
adults.option_b: The children represent the disciples
adults.option_c: Jesus was describing innocent children at play
adults.option_d: The marketplace represents the temple
adults.explanation: Luke 7:31-35 — "They are like unto children sitting in the marketplace... We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned to you, and ye have not wept." No style of ministry satisfied them — the problem was hardened hearts, not methods.

kids.question: In the parable of the sower, what does the good ground represent?
kids.option_a: People who hear God's word, keep it, and bring forth fruit
kids.option_b: People who never listen at all
kids.explanation: Luke 8:15 — "But that on the good ground are they, which in an honest and good heart, having heard the word, keep it, and bring forth fruit with patience."

kids.question: What did Jesus do when a big storm came while they were in a boat?
kids.option_a: He rebuked the wind and the raging water, and they stopped
kids.option_c: He asked the disciples to row faster
kids.explanation: Luke 8:24 — "And they came to him, and awoke him, saying, Master, master, we perish. Then he arose, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water: and they ceased, and there was a calm."

kids.question: What did the man with many demons say his name was?
kids.option_a: Legion, because many devils had entered him
kids.explanation: Luke 8:30 — "And Jesus asked him, saying, What is thy name? And he said, Legion: because many devils were entered into him."

teens.question: In the parable of the sower, what do the thorns represent?
teens.option_a: Cares, riches, and pleasures of this life which choke the word and prevent it from bearing fruit
teens.option_b: Good soil that helps plants grow
teens.option_c: Birds that eat the seed
teens.option_d: Rocks that block the sun
teens.explanation: Luke 8:14 — "And that which fell among thorns are they, which, when they have heard, go forth, and are choked with cares and riches and pleasures of this life, and bring no fruit to perfection."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to the disciples after calming the storm?
teens.option_a: Where is your faith? — and the disciples asked one another, What manner of man is this! for he commandeth even the winds and water, and they obey him
teens.option_b: You should have calmed the storm yourselves
teens.option_c: This storm was not dangerous at all
teens.option_d: You should have stayed on shore
teens.explanation: Luke 8:25 — "And he said unto them, Where is your faith? And they being afraid wondered, saying one to another, What manner of man is this! for he commandeth even the winds and water, and they obey him."

teens.question: What happened to the woman who had been bleeding for twelve years when she touched Jesus' garment?
teens.option_a: Her flow of blood stopped immediately — and Jesus said, Thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace
teens.option_b: She had to wait three more days to be healed
teens.option_c: Nothing happened at first
teens.option_d: Jesus did not notice her
teens.explanation: Luke 8:44,48 — "She came behind him, and touched the border of his garment: and immediately her issue of blood stanched... Daughter, be of good comfort: thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace."

teens.question: What did Jesus say when people laughed at Him for saying Jairus' daughter was not dead but sleeping?
teens.option_a: He put them all out, then took her by the hand and called, Maid, arise — and her spirit came again and she arose straightway
teens.option_b: He agreed that she was dead and left
teens.option_c: He got angry and refused to help
teens.option_d: He waited until the mourners finished
teens.explanation: Luke 8:54-55 — "And he put them all out, and took her by the hand, and called, saying, Maid, arise. And her spirit came again, and she arose straightway: and he commanded to give her meat."

teens.question: What did the healed demoniac want to do, and what did Jesus tell him instead?
teens.option_a: He wanted to stay with Jesus, but Jesus told him to return home and tell what great things God had done for him
teens.option_b: He wanted to go away to another country
teens.option_c: He wanted to become a fisherman
teens.option_d: He wanted to go to the temple in Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Luke 8:38-39 — "Now the man out of whom the devils were departed besought him that he might be with him: but Jesus sent him away, saying, Return to thine own house, and shew how great things God hath done unto thee."

adults.question: What is the purpose of parables according to Jesus' explanation in verses 9-10?
adults.option_a: To reveal truth to receptive hearts while concealing it from those who refuse to understand — "Unto you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God: but to others in parables; that seeing they might not see, and hearing they might not understand"
adults.option_b: To make teaching easier for everyone
adults.option_c: To entertain the crowds
adults.option_d: To avoid controversial topics
adults.explanation: Luke 8:10 — Jesus quotes Isaiah 6:9, explaining that parables function as both revelation and judgment — illuminating the willing and hardening the unwilling. This is a gracious judgment: light increases sight for those who see and blinds those who close their eyes.

adults.question: What four soil types in the parable represent four responses to God's word?
adults.option_a: Wayside (hard hearts, devil snatches the word), rock (superficial faith, no root in testing), thorns (choked by cares, riches, pleasures), good ground (honest hearts who bear fruit with patience)
adults.option_b: Four types of farming methods
adults.option_c: Four different crops
adults.option_d: Four seasons of the year
adults.explanation: Luke 8:12-15 — The four soils represent a spectrum of receptivity. Only the last brings forth fruit "with patience" — genuine faith endures testing, resists worldly distraction, and produces lasting results.

adults.question: What does Jesus' authority over the storm reveal about His nature?
adults.option_a: Power over creation belongs to God alone (cf. Psalm 107:29, Job 38:8-11) — by calming the sea with a command, Jesus demonstrates divine sovereignty over nature, prompting the Christological question: "What manner of man is this?"
adults.option_b: Jesus was a skilled sailor
adults.option_c: It was a coincidence that the storm stopped
adults.option_d: Jesus had natural weather knowledge
adults.explanation: Luke 8:24-25 — "He arose, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water: and they ceased." The OT consistently attributes mastery over seas and storms to YHWH alone. The disciples' astonishment is the right theological response.

adults.question: What is the significance of the name "Legion" in the Gadarene demoniac account?
adults.option_a: A Roman legion comprised 5,000-6,000 soldiers — the name indicates massive demonic possession, yet even this army of demons was subject to Jesus' authority and had to beg His permission before entering the swine
adults.option_b: It was the man's family name
adults.option_c: It was a common name in that region
adults.option_d: It referred to a local military unit
adults.explanation: Luke 8:30 — "Legion: because many devils were entered into him." The military metaphor emphasises both the magnitude of demonic bondage and the superiority of Christ's authority — He single-handedly routes an entire demonic army.

adults.question: Why did the Gadarenes ask Jesus to depart after the demoniac was healed?
adults.option_a: They were seized with great fear — the economic loss of the swine herd and the display of supernatural power frightened them more than the demoniac had, revealing that they valued their livelihood over liberation from evil
adults.option_b: They were happy and wanted to celebrate alone
adults.option_c: They had other business to attend to
adults.option_d: Jesus asked to leave first
adults.explanation: Luke 8:37 — "Then the whole multitude of the country of the Gadarenes round about besought him to depart from them; for they were taken with great fear." Fear of divine power, combined with economic concern, led them to reject the very one who could save them.

adults.question: What does the raising of Jairus' daughter alongside the healing of the woman with the issue of blood demonstrate about Jesus' power?
adults.option_a: Jesus' power is comprehensive — He can heal a chronic condition (12-year illness) and conquer death itself, and both miracles respond to faith, showing that no situation is beyond His ability
adults.option_b: These are unrelated stories placed together randomly
adults.option_c: Only one miracle actually happened
adults.option_d: Jesus could heal illness but not raise the dead
adults.explanation: Luke 8:43-55 — The intercalation (story-within-a-story) structure links these miracles: both involve 12 years (the woman's illness and the girl's age), both require faith, and together they show Jesus' authority over chronic disease and death itself.

adults.question: What does "No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel" teach about discipleship?
adults.option_a: Truth received must be shared openly — disciples are not to hide the mysteries of the kingdom but let them shine, because everything hidden will ultimately be revealed
adults.option_b: Candles should be stored carefully
adults.option_c: Only priests can teach spiritual truths
adults.option_d: Knowledge should be kept secret forever
adults.explanation: Luke 8:16-17 — "No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel, or putteth it under a bed; but setteth it on a candlestick, that they which enter in may see the light. For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest."

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement "My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it" redefine?
adults.option_a: Jesus was rejecting His biological family
adults.option_b: It redefines family in spiritual terms — biological kinship is secondary to the bond formed by hearing and obeying God's word, establishing the community of faith as the true family of God
adults.option_c: Jesus did not love His mother
adults.option_d: Family relationships have no value at all
adults.explanation: Luke 8:21 — "My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it." Jesus does not dishonour His family but elevates spiritual kinship through obedience above biological ties (cf. Luke 11:28).

kids.question: How many people did Jesus feed with five loaves and two fishes?
kids.option_a: About five thousand men, plus women and children
kids.explanation: Luke 9:14,16-17 — "For they were about five thousand men... Then he took the five loaves and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed them... And they did eat, and were all filled."

kids.question: What happened to Jesus on the mountain when He was praying?
kids.option_a: His face and clothes became shining white — He was transfigured
kids.option_b: He fell asleep on the mountain
kids.option_c: A rainbow appeared over His head
kids.option_d: He found a treasure hidden in a cave
kids.explanation: Luke 9:29 — "And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered, and his raiment was white and glistering."

kids.question: Who did Peter say Jesus was?
kids.option_a: The Christ of God — the promised Messiah
kids.explanation: Luke 9:20 — "He said unto them, But whom say ye that I am? Peter answering said, The Christ of God."

teens.question: What did Jesus say a person must do to follow Him?
teens.option_a: Deny himself, take up his cross daily, and follow me
teens.option_b: Bring a large donation of money
teens.option_c: First finish all worldly business
teens.option_d: Memorise the entire Old Testament
teens.explanation: Luke 9:23 — "And he said to them all, If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about saving and losing your life?
teens.option_a: Whosoever will save his life shall lose it: but whosoever will lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it
teens.option_b: Save your life at all costs
teens.option_c: Your physical life is all that matters
teens.option_d: Losing your life means losing everything
teens.explanation: Luke 9:24 — "For whosoever will save his life shall lose it: but whosoever will lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it."

teens.question: Who appeared with Jesus at the Transfiguration?
teens.option_a: Moses and Elias (Elijah), who spoke with Him about His departure which He would accomplish at Jerusalem
teens.option_b: Abraham and Isaac
teens.option_c: David and Solomon
teens.option_d: Adam and Noah
teens.explanation: Luke 9:30-31 — "And, behold, there talked with him two men, which were Moses and Elias: Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem."

teens.question: What did the voice from the cloud say at the Transfiguration?
teens.option_a: This is my beloved Son: hear him
teens.option_b: This is a great prophet among you
teens.option_c: This is the king of Israel
teens.option_d: This is the angel of the Lord
teens.explanation: Luke 9:35 — "And there came a voice out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the person who looks back after putting his hand to the plough?
teens.option_a: No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God
teens.option_b: Looking back is perfectly fine
teens.option_c: Only farmers need to worry about this
teens.option_d: Ploughing has nothing to do with following God
teens.explanation: Luke 9:62 — "And Jesus said unto him, No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God."

adults.question: What does the feeding of the five thousand reveal about Jesus in relation to Old Testament typology?
adults.option_a: Jesus acts as the new Moses who feeds God's people in the wilderness — and as the true Shepherd of Israel (Psalm 23, Ezekiel 34) who provides abundantly, with twelve baskets remaining (one for each tribe)
adults.option_b: It was simply a picnic
adults.option_c: Jesus was teaching cooking skills
adults.option_d: It was a normal meal with no deeper meaning
adults.explanation: Luke 9:16-17 — The miracle echoes God feeding Israel with manna (Exodus 16) and Elisha's multiplication (2 Kings 4:42-44). The twelve baskets of surplus symbolise God's provision for all Israel.

adults.question: Why did Moses and Elijah appear at the Transfiguration, and what is the significance of their topic of conversation?
adults.option_a: Moses represents the Law and Elijah the Prophets — both witnessing to Christ. They spoke of His "decease" (Greek: exodus) at Jerusalem, connecting the cross to Israel's founding deliverance from Egypt
adults.option_b: They appeared to congratulate Jesus
adults.option_c: They came to give Jesus new commandments
adults.option_d: It was a random supernatural event
adults.explanation: Luke 9:31 — "Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem." Luke uniquely uses "exodus" (departure), linking Jesus' death to the original Exodus — a greater deliverance from sin.

adults.question: What is the theological meaning of "take up his cross daily"?
adults.option_a: Daily self-denial and willingness to suffer for Christ — the cross was not a metaphor for minor inconvenience but an instrument of execution, meaning radical surrender of self-will to God's purposes
adults.option_b: Wearing a cross necklace every day
adults.option_c: Carrying a literal wooden cross
adults.option_d: Going to church on Sundays
adults.explanation: Luke 9:23 — "Let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me." Luke alone adds "daily," making it a continuous, repeated act of self-surrender rather than a single dramatic event.

adults.question: What does "For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and lose himself, or be cast away?" teach about priorities?
adults.option_a: The world has no value at all
adults.option_b: Gaining wealth is always wrong
