adults.option_c: No earthly gain can compensate for the loss of one's soul — the exchange rate between the world and the soul is infinite, making self-preservation at the cost of faithfulness the worst possible bargain
adults.option_d: This only applies to wealthy people
adults.explanation: Luke 9:25 — "For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and lose himself, or be cast away?" The rhetorical question exposes the folly of trading eternal life for temporal gain.

adults.question: What is significant about Luke 9:51 — "he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem"?
adults.option_a: It marks the beginning of Luke's extensive travel narrative (9:51–19:27) — Jesus deliberately and resolutely turns toward Jerusalem and the cross, showing His death was not an accident but a purposeful journey toward the Father's will
adults.option_b: Jesus was going to Jerusalem for a festival
adults.option_c: Jesus was looking for a place to rest
adults.option_d: Jesus was fleeing from Herod
adults.explanation: Luke 9:51 — "And it came to pass, when the time was come that he should be received up, he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem." This pivotal verse introduces the journey motif unique to Luke, comprising nearly ten chapters.

adults.question: Why did Jesus rebuke James and John for wanting to call down fire on the Samaritan village?
adults.option_a: Their desire for destruction contradicted Jesus' mission — "The Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them," correcting their vengeful Elijah-like impulse with the spirit of the gospel
adults.option_b: Jesus approved of their request
adults.option_c: The Samaritans deserved destruction
adults.option_d: James and John were completely right
adults.explanation: Luke 9:55-56 — "But he turned, and rebuked them, and said, Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of. For the Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them." Jesus corrects misguided zeal with mission clarity.

adults.question: What three would-be followers does Jesus encounter in verses 57-62, and what does each exchange teach?
adults.option_a: One offers eagerly (Jesus warns of homelessness); one is called but wants to bury his father (Jesus demands priority); one wants to say farewell first (Jesus warns against looking back) — total commitment with no conditions is required
adults.option_b: All three followed Jesus immediately
adults.option_c: Jesus rejected all three completely
adults.option_d: Only the first one was worthy
adults.explanation: Luke 9:57-62 — Three potential disciples illustrate obstacles: comfort (v.58), family obligations (v.59-60), and divided loyalty (v.61-62). Each exchange shows that discipleship demands unconditional, immediate, forward-looking commitment.

adults.question: What is the messianic secret, and how does Luke 9:21 illustrate it?
adults.option_a: Peter was wrong about Jesus being the Christ
adults.option_b: Jesus was unsure of His identity
adults.option_c: Jesus "straitly charged them, and commanded them to tell no man" that He was the Christ — He controlled the revelation of His identity to prevent political messianic expectations that would undermine His mission of suffering before glory
adults.option_d: Jesus wanted to avoid all public attention permanently
adults.explanation: Luke 9:21-22 — Jesus immediately follows Peter's confession with a passion prediction: the Christ must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and rise. He silences premature proclamation because the cross must precede the crown.

kids.question: In the story of the Good Samaritan, who stopped to help the hurt man?
kids.option_a: A Samaritan — a man from a group the Jews did not like
kids.option_c: A Levite who worked at the temple
kids.explanation: Luke 10:33 — "But a certain Samaritan, as he journeyed, came where he was: and when he saw him, he had compassion on him."

kids.question: What two sisters did Jesus visit in their home?
kids.option_a: Mary and Martha
kids.explanation: Luke 10:38-39 — "A certain woman named Martha received him into her house. And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word."

kids.question: What did Mary choose to do instead of helping Martha with the housework?
kids.option_a: She sat at Jesus' feet and listened to His word
kids.option_b: She went shopping at the market
kids.option_c: She took a nap in her room
kids.option_d: She went to visit a friend
kids.explanation: Luke 10:39 — "And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word."

teens.question: How many disciples did Jesus send out ahead of Him in pairs?
teens.option_a: Seventy (some manuscripts say seventy-two)
teens.option_b: Twelve
teens.option_c: Seven
teens.option_d: One hundred
teens.explanation: Luke 10:1 — "After these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself would come."

teens.question: What did Jesus tell the seventy to rejoice about?
teens.option_a: That their names are written in heaven — not that the spirits are subject to them
teens.option_b: That they had performed many miracles
teens.option_c: That they were famous among the people
teens.option_d: That they had defeated all their enemies
teens.explanation: Luke 10:20 — "Notwithstanding in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in heaven."

teens.question: What command summarises the whole law according to the lawyer's answer?
teens.option_a: Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, soul, strength, and mind; and thy neighbour as thyself
teens.option_b: Thou shalt not steal or kill
teens.option_c: Keep the Sabbath day holy
teens.option_d: Pay your tithes faithfully
teens.explanation: Luke 10:27 — "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength, and with all thy mind; and thy neighbour as thyself."

teens.question: In the Good Samaritan parable, who passed by the injured man without helping?
teens.option_a: A priest and a Levite — both religious leaders who should have shown mercy
teens.option_b: Two Roman soldiers
teens.option_c: A group of merchants
teens.option_d: The man's own family members
teens.explanation: Luke 10:31-32 — "And by chance there came down a certain priest that way: and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side. And likewise a Levite, when he was at the place, came and looked on him, and passed by on the other side."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to Martha when she complained that Mary was not helping?
teens.option_a: Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things: but one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her
teens.option_b: Martha, you are right to be upset
teens.option_c: Martha, tell Mary to come and help now
teens.option_d: Martha, stop cooking and rest
teens.explanation: Luke 10:41-42 — "And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things: But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Jesus sending seventy disciples, not just twelve?
adults.option_a: Seventy was a random number
adults.option_b: It was simply because more workers were needed
adults.option_c: The seventy likely symbolise the seventy nations of Genesis 10 — foreshadowing the universal mission to all peoples, while the twelve represented the twelve tribes of Israel. Luke emphasises the gospel's reach beyond Israel
adults.option_d: Jesus could not find enough qualified people for twelve
adults.explanation: Luke 10:1 — The number seventy echoes the seventy nations in the Table of Nations (Genesis 10) and the seventy elders appointed by Moses (Numbers 11:16-17). Luke, writing for a Gentile audience, highlights the universal scope of Christ's mission.

adults.question: What does "I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven" mean in context?
adults.option_a: As the seventy report victories over demons, Jesus sees the broader cosmic reality — Satan's authority is being overthrown through the disciples' ministry, anticipating the ultimate defeat of evil through the cross
adults.option_b: Satan literally fell from the sky that day
adults.option_c: Jesus was describing a past event with no present significance
adults.option_d: It was a metaphor about weather
adults.explanation: Luke 10:18 — "I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven." Jesus connects the disciples' exorcisms to a cosmic defeat of Satan. The imperfect tense ("I was watching") suggests an ongoing process of Satan's dethronement.

adults.question: Why is the Good Samaritan parable so shocking in its original context?
adults.option_a: The parable was not controversial at all
adults.option_b: Samaritans were widely respected by Jews
adults.option_c: Samaritans were despised by Jews as racial and religious half-breeds — making a Samaritan the hero while the priest and Levite (Israel's religious elite) fail, overturns every expectation and redefines "neighbour" beyond ethnic boundaries
adults.option_d: Priests always helped everyone in need
adults.explanation: Luke 10:33-37 — The Samaritan-Jewish hostility (cf. John 4:9, Luke 9:53) makes this parable explosive. Jesus demolishes ethno-religious tribalism: true neighbour-love transcends all boundaries. The lawyer cannot even say "Samaritan," only "he that shewed mercy."

adults.question: What is Jesus' response "Go, and do thou likewise" demanding of the lawyer?
adults.option_a: Practical, boundary-crossing compassion — not merely theological knowledge about who qualifies as a neighbour, but active mercy toward anyone in need, regardless of social, ethnic, or religious differences
adults.option_b: The lawyer should become a Samaritan
adults.option_c: The lawyer should travel the Jericho road
adults.option_d: The lawyer should give up studying the law
adults.explanation: Luke 10:37 — "Then said Jesus unto him, Go, and do thou likewise." The lawyer asked "Who is my neighbour?" (seeking to limit obligation). Jesus flips the question: "Which was neighbour?" — shifting from defining recipients to becoming an agent of mercy.

adults.question: What does the Mary and Martha story teach about priorities in discipleship?
adults.option_a: Sitting at Jesus' feet to hear His word is "the one thing needful" and "the good part" — active service is valuable but must not crowd out devotion to Christ and His teaching, which is the foundation of all ministry
adults.option_b: Martha's approach was wrong and sinful
adults.option_c: Housework should always be avoided
adults.option_d: Christians should never serve others practically
adults.explanation: Luke 10:42 — "One thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her." Jesus does not condemn service but corrects anxious busyness that displaces communion with Him.

adults.question: What does Jesus' prayer in verses 21-22 reveal about divine revelation?
adults.option_a: God hides truth from the wise and prudent and reveals it to babes — salvation knowledge comes not through human intellect but through divine disclosure, and only the Son fully knows the Father
adults.option_b: Only scholars can understand spiritual truth
adults.option_c: Education is the key to knowing God
adults.option_d: Human wisdom is sufficient for salvation
adults.explanation: Luke 10:21-22 — "Thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes... no man knoweth who the Son is, but the Father; and who the Father is, but the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal him."

adults.question: What Christological claim is embedded in verse 22?
adults.option_a: Jesus claims exclusive mutual knowledge with the Father — "no man knoweth who the Son is, but the Father; and who the Father is, but the Son" — an extraordinary claim to unique divine relationship and authority as the sole mediator of knowledge of God
adults.option_b: Jesus was simply a great teacher
adults.option_c: Anyone can know God equally well
adults.option_d: This verse makes no special claim about Jesus
adults.explanation: Luke 10:22 — This verse, sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" because it sounds like John's Gospel, reveals the Son's unique filial relationship with the Father and His exclusive role as revealer of God.

adults.question: What does "Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see" tell the disciples about their privileged position?
adults.option_a: They are witnessing what prophets and kings longed to see but could not — the fulfilment of messianic promises in their very presence, making them the most privileged generation in redemptive history
adults.option_b: The disciples had better eyesight than others
adults.option_c: All generations are equally blessed
adults.option_d: This only applies to the seventy, not others
adults.explanation: Luke 10:23-24 — "Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see: For I tell you, that many prophets and kings have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them." The disciples witness the dawn of the messianic age.

kids.question: What prayer did Jesus teach His disciples?
kids.option_a: The Lord's Prayer — Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name
kids.option_b: A prayer about food and clothing
kids.explanation: Luke 11:2 — "And he said unto them, When ye pray, say, Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth."

kids.question: What did Jesus say would happen if you ask, seek, and knock?
kids.option_a: Ask and it shall be given you; seek and ye shall find; knock and it shall be opened unto you
kids.option_c: You must wait a very long time
kids.option_d: Only grown-ups can ask God for things
kids.explanation: Luke 11:9 — "And I say unto you, Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you."

kids.question: What good gift does the heavenly Father give to those who ask Him?
kids.option_a: The Holy Spirit
kids.explanation: Luke 11:13 — "If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children: how much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to them that ask him?"

teens.question: What point did Jesus make with the parable of the friend at midnight?
teens.option_a: Because of persistent asking (importunity), the friend will give what is needed — teaching that we should persist in prayer without giving up
teens.option_b: Friends should never bother each other
teens.option_c: Only ask God once and never again
teens.option_d: Midnight prayers are more powerful
teens.explanation: Luke 11:8 — "Though he will not rise and give him, because he is his friend, yet because of his importunity he will rise and give him as many as he needeth."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about a kingdom divided against itself?
teens.option_a: Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation — He used this to refute the claim that He cast out devils by Beelzebub
teens.option_b: Divided kingdoms are always stronger
teens.option_c: Kingdoms never divide
teens.option_d: Only earthly kingdoms can be divided
teens.explanation: Luke 11:17 — "Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and a house divided against a house falleth."

teens.question: What did Jesus say is the only sign that would be given to that generation?
teens.option_a: The sign of Jonas the prophet — as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so shall the Son of man be to this generation
teens.option_b: A star appearing in the sky
teens.option_c: The temple being rebuilt
teens.option_d: A military victory over Rome
teens.explanation: Luke 11:29-30 — "This is an evil generation: they seek a sign; and there shall no sign be given it, but the sign of Jonas the prophet. For as Jonas was a sign unto the Ninevites, so shall also the Son of man be to this generation."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the Pharisees and their concern for outward cleanliness?
teens.option_a: Ye Pharisees make clean the outside of the cup and platter, but your inward part is full of ravening and wickedness
teens.option_b: The Pharisees were perfectly clean inside and outside
teens.option_c: Outward cleanliness is all that matters
teens.option_d: Jesus praised their washing habits
teens.explanation: Luke 11:39 — "Now do ye Pharisees make clean the outside of the cup and the platter; but your inward part is full of ravening and wickedness."

teens.question: Who did Jesus say are truly blessed?
teens.option_a: Blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it
teens.option_b: Blessed are those who are wealthy and powerful
teens.option_c: Blessed are those who are famous
teens.option_d: Blessed are those who never face difficulty
teens.explanation: Luke 11:28 — "But he said, Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it."

adults.question: How does Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer differ from Matthew's, and what is significant?
adults.option_a: Luke's version is shorter and emphasises daily dependence ("give us day by day our daily bread") and the Holy Spirit as the gift of prayer (v.13) — whereas Matthew's is more liturgical. Luke shows prayer as personal communion with a generous Father
adults.option_b: They are identical
adults.option_c: Luke's version is much longer
adults.option_d: Matthew did not include the Lord's Prayer
adults.explanation: Luke 11:2-4 — Luke's shorter form may represent the prayer's essence, while Matthew's expanded version was used liturgically. Luke uniquely concludes the teaching with the promise of the Holy Spirit (v.13), the Father's supreme gift.

adults.question: What does the "finger of God" reference mean when Jesus says "I with the finger of God cast out devils"?
adults.option_a: It alludes to Exodus 8:19 where the Egyptian magicians recognised God's power — Jesus claims the same divine power that delivered Israel from Egypt is now active in His exorcisms, proving the kingdom of God has come
adults.option_b: It refers to a magical technique
adults.option_c: It means Jesus used physical force
adults.option_d: It has no Old Testament background
adults.explanation: Luke 11:20 — "But if I with the finger of God cast out devils, no doubt the kingdom of God is come upon you." Matthew 12:28 has "Spirit of God." Luke's "finger of God" echoes Exodus 8:19, linking Jesus' exorcisms to the Exodus deliverance.

adults.question: What does the parable of the strong man and the stronger man teach about Jesus' ministry?
adults.option_a: Jesus was the strong man, not the stronger one
adults.option_b: Satan and Jesus are equally powerful
adults.option_c: The strong man represents the Roman Empire
adults.option_d: Satan is the strong man guarding his palace; Jesus is the stronger one who overcomes him, takes away his armour, and divides his spoils — exorcisms are evidence of Satan's defeat, not collusion with him
adults.explanation: Luke 11:21-22 — "When a strong man armed keepeth his palace, his goods are in peace: But when a stronger than he shall come upon him, and overcome him, he taketh from him all his armour wherein he trusted, and divideth his spoils."

adults.question: What does the sign of Jonah mean in Luke's context?
adults.option_a: Just as Jonah's preaching was itself the sign to Nineveh (not the fish episode), so Jesus' preaching and person are the sign to this generation — if they reject the greater-than-Jonah, their judgment will be more severe
adults.option_b: Jonah and Jesus both spent time inside a fish
adults.option_c: The sign was about going on a sea voyage
adults.option_d: There is no connection between Jonah and Jesus
adults.explanation: Luke 11:30,32 — "For as Jonas was a sign unto the Ninevites, so shall also the Son of man be to this generation... The men of Nineve shall rise up in the judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: for they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here."

adults.question: What does Jesus' woe against the lawyers for taking away "the key of knowledge" mean?
adults.option_a: The religious experts had so distorted Scripture with their traditions that they prevented people from understanding God's truth — they neither entered the kingdom themselves nor allowed others to enter
adults.option_b: Lawyers had stolen physical keys from the temple
adults.option_c: Education in general was being criticised
adults.option_d: Only lawyers could understand the Bible
adults.explanation: Luke 11:52 — "Woe unto you, lawyers! for ye have taken away the key of knowledge: ye entered not in yourselves, and them that were entering in ye hindered." The "key of knowledge" is the correct interpretation of Scripture that opens the door to God's kingdom.

adults.question: What does the warning about the unclean spirit returning with seven others teach?
adults.option_a: Mere moral reform without being filled with God's presence leaves a person worse off — the empty, swept house represents a life cleansed of evil but not filled with good, making it vulnerable to worse spiritual bondage
adults.option_b: Demons always travel in groups of seven
adults.option_c: Houses should never be cleaned
adults.option_d: This parable is about hygiene practices
adults.explanation: Luke 11:24-26 — "Then goeth he, and taketh to him seven other spirits more wicked than himself; and they enter in, and dwell there: and the last state of that man is worse than the first." Spiritual vacuum invites worse occupation.

adults.question: What did Jesus mean by "the queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation"?
adults.option_a: The queen of the south was from Rome
adults.option_b: The queen will literally sit as a judge
adults.option_c: This refers to a future female ruler
adults.option_d: The Queen of Sheba, a Gentile, will condemn Israel because she travelled far to hear Solomon's wisdom, yet Israel rejects one greater than Solomon standing in their midst — judgment is proportional to privilege
adults.explanation: Luke 11:31 — "The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation, and condemn them: for she came from the utmost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here."

adults.question: What does Jesus' accusation that the Pharisees "build the sepulchres of the prophets" mean?
adults.option_a: They honour dead prophets while persecuting living ones — by building tombs for prophets their fathers killed, they complete their fathers' work and consent to the same pattern of rejecting God's messengers
adults.option_b: Jesus was criticising funeral customs
adults.option_c: Building tombs was a righteous act
adults.option_d: The Pharisees truly honoured the prophets
adults.explanation: Luke 11:47-48 — "Woe unto you! for ye build the sepulchres of the prophets, and your fathers killed them. Truly ye bear witness that ye allow the deeds of your fathers: for they indeed killed them, and ye build their sepulchres." Honouring dead prophets while rejecting living ones exposes hypocrisy.

kids.question: What did God say to the rich man who built bigger barns to store his grain?
kids.option_a: Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee
kids.option_b: Well done, you are very clever
kids.explanation: Luke 12:20 — "But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?"

kids.question: What did Jesus say about sparrows and how much God cares for us?
kids.option_a: You are worth more than many sparrows — God knows and cares for every one of them, and He cares even more for you
kids.option_b: Sparrows are more important than people
kids.option_c: God does not care about birds
kids.explanation: Luke 12:6-7 — "Are not five sparrows sold for two farthings, and not one of them is forgotten before God? ... Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows."

kids.question: Where did Jesus say our treasure should be?
kids.option_a: In heaven, where no thief comes near and no moth destroys
kids.option_d: In a treasure chest at home
kids.explanation: Luke 12:33-34 — "Provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth. For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also."

teens.question: What did Jesus warn about the leaven (yeast) of the Pharisees?
teens.option_a: Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy — their false outward religion spreads like yeast through dough
teens.option_b: The Pharisees made excellent bread
teens.option_c: Yeast is always good and useful
teens.option_d: The Pharisees were honest people
teens.explanation: Luke 12:1 — "Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about covetousness?
teens.option_a: Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth
teens.option_b: Having many possessions makes you happy
teens.option_c: The more you own, the better your life
teens.option_d: Wealth always shows God's approval
teens.explanation: Luke 12:15 — "And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about worry and the lilies of the field?
teens.option_a: Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these — if God clothes the grass, He will clothe you
teens.option_b: We should worry about clothes all the time
teens.option_c: Only rich people can stop worrying
teens.option_d: Lilies are better than people
teens.explanation: Luke 12:27 — "Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about being ready for His return?
teens.option_a: Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not
teens.option_b: There is no need to be ready yet
teens.option_c: Only the disciples need to be ready
teens.option_d: Jesus will announce His return in advance
teens.explanation: Luke 12:40 — "Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not."

teens.question: What principle did Jesus teach about responsibility?
teens.option_a: For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required — greater privilege brings greater accountability
teens.option_b: Those who have more can do whatever they want
teens.option_c: Responsibility decreases with more gifts
teens.option_d: Only leaders are held accountable
teens.explanation: Luke 12:48 — "For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more."

adults.question: What does the parable of the Rich Fool teach about the relationship between wealth and security?
adults.option_a: Material wealth provides no security before God — the man's soul was required that very night, exposing the folly of storing up treasures for self while being "not rich toward God"
adults.option_b: Wealth guarantees a long and happy life
adults.option_c: Building bigger barns is always wise
adults.option_d: God approves of all wealthy people
adults.explanation: Luke 12:20-21 — "Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee... So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God." The parable exposes the illusion that material security equals existential security.

adults.question: What does "where your treasure is, there will your heart be also" reveal about the human condition?
adults.option_a: Treasure has no influence on the heart
adults.option_b: Hearts naturally go wherever they want
adults.option_c: Our deepest affections follow our investments — treasure placement determines heart orientation, not the reverse. Jesus teaches that we can redirect our hearts by redirecting our resources toward heavenly purposes
adults.option_d: This only applies to wealthy people
adults.explanation: Luke 12:34 — "For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also." Jesus does not say "where your heart is, there your treasure will be" — He says treasure leads the heart. Strategic generosity reorients affection.

adults.question: What does "Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom" reveal about God's disposition?
adults.option_a: The flock must earn the kingdom
adults.option_b: The kingdom is only for large groups
adults.option_c: God reluctantly gives the kingdom
adults.option_d: Despite the disciples' smallness and vulnerability, God delights in giving them the kingdom — the contrast between "little flock" and the grandeur of "the kingdom" shows that God's grace is lavish and His purposes are not limited by human weakness
adults.explanation: Luke 12:32 — "Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom." The tender diminutive "little flock" combined with the Father's "good pleasure" (eudokia) reveals gracious divine generosity.

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement about bringing "not peace, but division" mean in context?
adults.option_a: The gospel inevitably creates division even within families — allegiance to Christ may conflict with family loyalty, not because Jesus seeks conflict but because His claims demand a response that splits households along lines of faith and unbelief
adults.option_b: Jesus promotes family conflict
adults.option_c: Christianity should never cause any disagreement
adults.option_d: Division only happens among strangers
adults.explanation: Luke 12:51-53 — "Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three." The gospel's absolute claims create unavoidable lines of division.

adults.question: What does the faithful and wise steward parable teach about accountability in leadership?
adults.option_a: Only the unfaithful steward exists
adults.option_b: All leaders will be treated the same regardless
adults.option_c: Leaders who faithfully serve during the master's absence will be rewarded with greater responsibility, while those who abuse their authority will be severely punished — privilege increases accountability
adults.option_d: The master will never return
adults.explanation: Luke 12:42-46 — "Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household... Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing." The contrast between faithful and unfaithful stewards illustrates degrees of reward and judgment.

adults.question: What does "unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required" establish as a principle of divine judgment?
adults.option_a: Judgment is proportional to privilege — those with greater knowledge, gifts, and opportunities face stricter accountability. The servant who knew his lord's will and did not do it is beaten with many stripes, while the ignorant servant receives fewer
adults.option_b: Everyone is judged by the same standard regardless
adults.option_c: Ignorance is always a valid excuse
adults.option_d: Only knowledge without action is judged
adults.explanation: Luke 12:47-48 — Two levels of punishment based on knowledge illustrate that culpability scales with awareness. This has implications for those who hear the gospel clearly versus those with less exposure.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "I am come to send fire on the earth"?
adults.option_a: Jesus will literally set the earth on fire
adults.option_b: The fire represents the refining, purifying, and dividing work of the gospel — and Jesus' baptism refers to His impending suffering on the cross, which He is "straitened" (distressed) until it is accomplished
adults.option_c: Fire refers to military conquest
adults.option_d: This passage has no deeper meaning
adults.explanation: Luke 12:49-50 — "I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled? But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished!" Fire represents judgment/purification, and the "baptism" is His passion.

adults.question: What does Jesus' rebuke about discerning the weather but not "this time" imply?
adults.option_a: The religious leaders could interpret natural signs (clouds from the west mean rain, south wind means heat) but wilfully refused to read the spiritual signs of the times — the Messiah was in their midst and they could not or would not recognise Him
adults.option_b: Jesus was teaching meteorology
adults.option_c: Only weather experts could understand
adults.option_d: The people had no access to spiritual information
adults.explanation: Luke 12:54-56 — "Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time?" The charge of hypocrisy implies wilful blindness rather than inability — they chose not to see.

kids.question: What did Jesus say would happen to people who do not repent?
kids.option_a: They shall all likewise perish — Jesus warned everyone to turn from sin
kids.option_b: Nothing bad will happen to them
kids.option_d: They will live forever without God
kids.explanation: Luke 13:3 — "I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish."

kids.question: What did Jesus compare the kingdom of God to?
kids.option_a: A mustard seed that grows into a great tree where birds nest in the branches
kids.option_b: A big castle with gold walls
kids.option_c: A deep ocean full of fish
kids.option_d: A tall mountain covered with snow
kids.explanation: Luke 13:19 — "It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and cast into his garden; and it grew, and waxed a great tree; and the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it."

kids.question: What did Jesus do for the woman who had been bent over for eighteen years?
kids.option_a: He healed her and she stood up straight and praised God
kids.option_b: He told her to wait until tomorrow
kids.option_c: He asked the Pharisees to help her
kids.explanation: Luke 13:12-13 — "And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity. And he laid his hands on her: and immediately she was made straight, and glorified God."

teens.question: What did the ruler of the synagogue complain about when Jesus healed on the Sabbath?
teens.option_a: He said there are six days to work and people should come to be healed on those days, not the Sabbath
teens.option_b: He was happy that Jesus healed the woman
teens.option_c: He asked Jesus to heal more people
teens.option_d: He invited Jesus to come back next Sabbath
teens.explanation: Luke 13:14 — "And the ruler of the synagogue answered with indignation, because that Jesus had healed on the sabbath day, and said unto the people, There are six days in which men ought to work: in them therefore come and be healed, and not on the sabbath day."

teens.question: What did Jesus call the religious leaders who opposed Sabbath healing?
teens.option_a: Hypocrites — because they would untie their ox or donkey on the Sabbath to give it water, but objected to freeing a daughter of Abraham from Satan's bondage
teens.option_b: Faithful followers of the law
teens.option_c: Wise teachers of Israel
teens.option_d: True servants of God
teens.explanation: Luke 13:15 — "Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?"

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the gate that leads to salvation?
teens.option_a: Strive to enter in at the strait gate: for many will seek to enter in, and shall not be able
teens.option_b: The gate is wide and everyone can fit through
teens.option_c: There is no gate at all
teens.option_d: Only Jews can enter the gate
teens.explanation: Luke 13:24 — "Strive to enter in at the strait gate: for many, I say unto you, will seek to enter in, and shall not be able."

teens.question: What reversal did Jesus predict about who would be in the kingdom?
teens.option_a: There are last which shall be first, and there are first which shall be last — Gentiles will come from east, west, north, and south while some expected members will be excluded
teens.option_b: The wealthy will always be first
teens.option_c: No reversal will ever happen
teens.option_d: Only the first will remain first
teens.explanation: Luke 13:29-30 — "And they shall come from the east, and from the west, and from the north, and from the south, and shall sit down in the kingdom of God. And, behold, there are last which shall be first, and there are first which shall be last."

teens.question: What did Jesus lament about Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, which killest the prophets — how often would I have gathered thy children together as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, and ye would not
teens.option_b: Jerusalem is the greatest city in the world
teens.option_c: Jerusalem always welcomes God's prophets
teens.option_d: Jerusalem needs better walls and defences
teens.explanation: Luke 13:34 — "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, which killest the prophets, and stonest them that are sent unto thee; how often would I have gathered thy children together, as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, and ye would not!"

adults.question: What theological error does Jesus correct when asked about the Galileans whose blood Pilate mingled with their sacrifices?
adults.option_a: He rejects the assumption that suffering indicates greater sinfulness — those who suffered were not worse sinners than others, but all must repent or face a similar fate. Tragedy is a call to repentance, not a basis for judgment of others
adults.option_b: Those who suffer are always being punished for sin
adults.option_c: Only innocent people suffer
adults.option_d: Suffering has no spiritual significance
adults.explanation: Luke 13:2-3 — "Suppose ye that these Galilaeans were sinners above all the Galilaeans, because they suffered such things? I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish." Jesus refutes retributive theology while urging universal repentance.

adults.question: What does the parable of the barren fig tree teach about God's patience and judgment?
adults.option_a: God grants a season of grace (the extra year of digging and dunging) but patience has a limit — the tree that fails to bear fruit after extended opportunity will ultimately be cut down. Mercy delays judgment but does not cancel it
adults.option_b: God will wait forever with no consequences
adults.option_c: The fig tree represents the temple only
adults.option_d: Judgment is never coming
adults.explanation: Luke 13:6-9 — "Lord, let it alone this year also, till I shall dig about it, and dung it: And if it bear fruit, well: and if not, then after that thou shalt cut it down." The vinedresser (Christ) intercedes for more time, but fruitlessness will ultimately bring judgment.

adults.question: What does Jesus' healing of the woman bent over for eighteen years reveal about the Sabbath's purpose?
adults.option_a: The Sabbath was made for the liberation and blessing of God's people — Jesus calls her a "daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound," reframing healing as release from bondage, which perfectly fulfils the Sabbath's redemptive intent
adults.option_b: The Sabbath prohibits all activity
adults.option_c: Healing violates the Sabbath law
adults.option_d: Only emergencies justify Sabbath work
adults.explanation: Luke 13:16 — "And ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the sabbath day?" Jesus frames Sabbath healing as Sabbath liberation — the very purpose of the day.

adults.question: What does the parable of the mustard seed teach about the kingdom's growth pattern?
adults.option_a: The kingdom begins insignificantly (like the smallest seed) but grows to surprising magnitude (a great tree) — God's kingdom starts with a small band of disciples but will grow to encompass people from all nations
adults.option_b: The kingdom will always remain small
adults.option_c: Only large movements matter to God
adults.option_d: The kingdom grows through military conquest
adults.explanation: Luke 13:18-19 — "Unto what is the kingdom of God like?... It is like a grain of mustard seed... and it grew, and waxed a great tree." The contrast between the tiny seed and the great tree mirrors the contrast between Jesus' small following and the future worldwide church.

adults.question: What does "Lord, Lord, open unto us" and the response "I know you not whence ye are" teach about false assurance?
adults.option_a: Superficial association with Jesus (eating in His presence, hearing Him teach) is insufficient — genuine relationship requires entering through the narrow gate of repentance and obedience, not presuming on proximity
adults.option_b: Simply being near Jesus guarantees salvation
adults.option_c: Everyone who calls on the Lord will be saved regardless
adults.option_d: Knowledge of Jesus' teaching is enough
adults.explanation: Luke 13:25-27 — "Lord, Lord, open unto us... I know you not whence ye are; depart from me, all ye workers of iniquity." These people had proximity to Jesus but not genuine relationship. Acquaintance is not the same as discipleship.

adults.question: What does Jesus' response to the warning about Herod — "Go ye, and tell that fox" — reveal?
adults.option_a: Jesus was afraid of Herod
adults.option_b: Jesus shows fearless defiance of political threats — calling Herod a fox (cunning but insignificant compared to God's purposes), and declaring that His ministry timeline is determined by divine necessity, not human threats
adults.option_c: Jesus planned to flee from Herod
adults.option_d: Jesus wanted to make peace with Herod
adults.explanation: Luke 13:32 — "Go ye, and tell that fox, Behold, I cast out devils, and I do cures to day and to morrow, and the third day I shall be perfected." Jesus asserts sovereign control over His own schedule, implying He will die in Jerusalem on God's timetable, not Herod's.

adults.question: What does the lament over Jerusalem reveal about divine sovereignty and human responsibility?
adults.option_a: Jesus wept because He desired to gather Jerusalem's children but they would not — showing genuine divine desire for their salvation alongside their responsible rejection. Both God's will to save and human will to refuse are affirmed
adults.option_b: God does not care about Jerusalem
adults.option_c: The people had no choice in the matter
adults.option_d: Jerusalem was always obedient
adults.explanation: Luke 13:34 — "How often would I have gathered thy children together... and ye would not!" The tension between "I would" and "ye would not" holds together divine compassion and human culpability without resolving the mystery.

adults.question: What does "your house is left unto you desolate" prophetically anticipate?
adults.option_a: The destruction of the temple in AD 70 — because Israel rejected the Messiah, the temple (their "house") would be abandoned by God's presence, leading to its physical destruction by the Romans
adults.option_b: A minor renovation of the temple
adults.option_c: The temple would last forever
adults.option_d: The house refers to a private home
adults.explanation: Luke 13:35 — "Behold, your house is left unto you desolate." This echoes Jeremiah 22:5 and anticipates the temple's destruction. "Your house" shifts from "my house" (Luke 2:49) — God's departure precedes physical destruction.

kids.question: What did Jesus say about choosing the best seat at a dinner?
kids.option_a: Sit in the lowest place so the host can invite you higher — those who humble themselves will be lifted up
kids.option_b: Always grab the best seat first
kids.option_c: Fight for the seat closest to the host
kids.option_d: It does not matter where you sit
kids.explanation: Luke 14:10-11 — "Go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, Friend, go up higher... For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted."

kids.question: In the parable of the great supper, what excuses did the invited guests make?
kids.option_a: One bought a field, one bought oxen, and one had just gotten married — they all missed the feast
kids.option_b: They were all too sick to come
kids.option_c: They came early and ate everything
kids.option_d: They brought better food to share
kids.explanation: Luke 14:18-20 — The three excuses — buying land, buying oxen, and a new marriage — represent worldly priorities that crowd out God's invitation.

kids.question: Who did the master invite to the feast after the first guests refused?
kids.option_a: The poor, the maimed, the halt, and the blind from the streets and lanes
kids.explanation: Luke 14:21 — "Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind."

teens.question: What principle did Jesus teach about being exalted and humbled?
teens.option_a: Whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted
teens.option_b: The proud always succeed
teens.option_c: Humility is weakness
teens.option_d: Only strong people are exalted
teens.explanation: Luke 14:11 — "For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted."

teens.question: Who did Jesus say you should invite when you host a feast?
teens.option_a: The poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind — those who cannot repay you, and you shall be recompensed at the resurrection of the just
teens.option_b: Only your friends and rich neighbours
teens.option_c: People who can repay you with bigger feasts
teens.option_d: Only important community leaders
teens.explanation: Luke 14:13-14 — "But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind: And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about counting the cost before following Him?
teens.option_a: Like a man building a tower who first counts the cost to see if he can finish, or a king going to war who considers if he can win — discipleship requires counting the cost
teens.option_b: Just follow without thinking
teens.option_c: The cost is always zero
teens.option_d: Only rich people need to count the cost
teens.explanation: Luke 14:28-31 — "For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?"

teens.question: What shocking statement did Jesus make about family relationships and discipleship?
teens.option_a: If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple
teens.option_b: You must always put family before Jesus
teens.option_c: Family is more important than anything
teens.option_d: Jesus never asked anyone to leave their family
teens.explanation: Luke 14:26 — "If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple." "Hate" is a Semitic idiom meaning "love less by comparison."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about salt that has lost its flavour?
teens.option_a: It is neither fit for the land nor for the dunghill — men cast it out. Salt without saltiness is useless
teens.option_b: It can easily regain its flavour
teens.option_c: It becomes sweeter
teens.option_d: It is still useful for cooking
teens.explanation: Luke 14:34-35 — "Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned? It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out."

adults.question: What does the parable of the great supper teach about Israel's rejection and the inclusion of outsiders?
adults.option_a: The originally invited guests (Israel's religious elite) refuse the invitation through worldly excuses, so the master sends servants to bring in the marginalized (poor, maimed, blind) and then Gentiles from highways and hedges — the kingdom is filled with unexpected guests
adults.option_b: All the invited guests attended happily
adults.option_c: Only the original guests could come
adults.option_d: The feast was cancelled
adults.explanation: Luke 14:16-24 — The two rounds of invitation (streets/lanes of the city, then highways/hedges) likely represent outreach first to marginalized Jews, then to Gentiles. "None of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper" is a solemn warning.

adults.question: How does "hate" in verse 26 function as a Semitic idiom, and what does it actually demand?
adults.option_a: In Hebrew idiom, "hate" means "love less" by comparison — it demands supreme loyalty to Jesus above every human relationship, not literal hatred of family. Genesis 29:31 uses the same idiom: Leah was "hated" (loved less) compared to Rachel
adults.option_b: Jesus commands literal hatred of all family
adults.option_c: Family should be completely abandoned
adults.option_d: This verse only applies to monks and nuns
adults.explanation: Luke 14:26 — Compare Matthew 10:37 which clarifies: "He that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me." The Semitic comparative idiom places Christ's claim as absolute, surpassing all other loyalties.

adults.question: What do the twin parables of the tower builder and the warring king teach about discipleship?
adults.option_a: Both parables urge sober assessment before committing — discipleship is not a casual decision but a total life commitment that should be entered with eyes wide open about its demands, lest one start but fail to finish
adults.option_b: Jesus was teaching architecture and military strategy
adults.option_c: These parables discourage people from following Jesus
adults.option_d: Only wealthy people can be disciples
adults.explanation: Luke 14:28-33 — The tower builder who cannot finish faces ridicule; the king who cannot win must negotiate peace. Both analogies warn against superficial commitment and lead to verse 33: "Whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple."

adults.question: What is the literary function of the three excuses in the great supper parable?
adults.option_a: They represent legitimate worldly concerns (property, business, family) that become sinful when they take priority over God's invitation — the excuses escalate in intensity (field, oxen, marriage) and all begin with "I" (self-centred refusal)
adults.option_b: The excuses were all completely valid
adults.option_c: Only one excuse was unreasonable
adults.option_d: The host should have chosen a better date
adults.explanation: Luke 14:18-20 — Each excuse involves a good thing (land, livelihood, marriage) elevated above the ultimate good. Deuteronomy 20:5-7 lists similar exemptions from war — what excuses a soldier from battle does not excuse one from God's banquet.

adults.question: What does "compel them to come in" mean in the context of the parable?
adults.option_a: The urgency and earnestness of the gospel invitation — the master wants his house filled and insists that even those from the highways and hedges (the most unlikely guests) be persuaded to come. It speaks to God's passionate desire that none miss His grace
adults.option_b: Physical force should be used in evangelism
adults.option_c: People should be forced to become Christians
adults.option_d: Only certain people are worthy of invitation
adults.explanation: Luke 14:23 — "Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled." The "compulsion" is persuasive urgency (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:11, "we persuade men"), not physical coercion.

adults.question: What does Jesus' silence of the Pharisees regarding Sabbath healing of the man with dropsy demonstrate?
adults.option_a: The Pharisees could not answer Jesus' question "Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?" because any answer would undermine their position — their silence reveals the indefensibility of opposing mercy on the Sabbath
adults.option_b: The Pharisees agreed with Jesus
adults.option_c: The Pharisees simply did not hear the question
adults.option_d: Jesus avoided confrontation with the Pharisees
adults.explanation: Luke 14:3-6 — "Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day? And they held their peace... Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day? And they could not answer him again to these things."

adults.question: What does the instruction to invite the poor rather than the rich to your feast teach about the kingdom's economy?
adults.option_a: The kingdom operates on grace, not reciprocity — inviting those who cannot repay mirrors God's own generosity toward undeserving sinners and will be rewarded "at the resurrection of the just," shifting the reward timeline from earthly to eternal
adults.option_b: Only poor people should eat together
adults.option_c: Hospitality to friends is wrong
adults.option_d: This teaching has no practical application
adults.explanation: Luke 14:12-14 — Jesus disrupts the honour-shame patronage system of ancient culture, where banquets were investments in social capital. Kingdom hospitality serves without expectation, trusting God for the return.

adults.question: What does the salt metaphor at the end of chapter 14 warn disciples about?
adults.option_a: Disciples who lose their distinctive commitment become useless to the kingdom — just as salt without savour serves no purpose (not even as fertilizer), half-hearted discipleship benefits neither God nor the world
adults.option_b: Salt is only for cooking
adults.option_c: This warning applies only to the twelve apostles
adults.option_d: Salt never actually loses its flavour
adults.explanation: Luke 14:34-35 — "Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?" In context, following the cost-of-discipleship teaching, salt represents wholehearted commitment. Compromise renders a disciple worthless.

kids.question: In the parable of the lost sheep, what did the shepherd do when one sheep was lost?
kids.option_a: He left the ninety-nine and went after the one lost sheep until he found it
kids.option_b: He forgot about the lost sheep
kids.option_c: He sent someone else to look for it
kids.option_d: He counted the ninety-nine and was satisfied
kids.explanation: Luke 15:4 — "What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it?"

kids.question: In the story of the prodigal son, what happened to the young man after he spent all his money?
kids.option_a: He was so hungry he wanted to eat the pigs' food
kids.option_b: He found a better job in the far country
kids.explanation: Luke 15:15-16 — "And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine. And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him."

kids.question: What did the father do when he saw his lost son coming home?
kids.option_a: He ran to him, hugged him, and kissed him
kids.option_b: He turned him away at the door
kids.option_d: He sent a servant to talk to him
kids.explanation: Luke 15:20 — "But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him."

teens.question: What happens in heaven when one sinner repents?
teens.option_a: There is joy in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons which need no repentance
teens.option_b: Nothing special happens
teens.option_c: Only the angels notice
teens.option_d: Heaven waits until many sinners repent at once
teens.explanation: Luke 15:7 — "I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance."

teens.question: What did the prodigal son plan to say to his father?
teens.option_a: Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee, and am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants
teens.option_b: Father, give me more money
teens.option_c: Father, I did nothing wrong
teens.option_d: Father, my brother caused all my problems
teens.explanation: Luke 15:18-19 — "I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee, And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants."

teens.question: What did the father give the returning prodigal son?
teens.option_a: The best robe, a ring for his hand, shoes for his feet, and a fatted calf for a feast
teens.option_b: A list of rules he must follow
teens.option_c: A small room in the servants' quarters
teens.option_d: Nothing until he proved himself
teens.explanation: Luke 15:22-23 — "Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet: And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry."

teens.question: Why was the older brother angry?
teens.option_a: He was upset that his father threw a feast for the returning prodigal while he had faithfully served for years and never received even a young goat
teens.option_b: He was happy his brother returned
teens.option_c: He was angry about the weather
teens.option_d: He was upset about his own work
teens.explanation: Luke 15:28-30 — "And he was angry, and would not go in... Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends."

teens.question: What did the father say about the prodigal's return?
teens.option_a: This my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found
teens.option_b: My son must earn back my trust first
teens.option_c: My son deserves punishment before forgiveness
teens.option_d: My son should have stayed away longer
teens.explanation: Luke 15:24 — "For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry."

adults.question: What is the narrative context that prompted Jesus to tell the three parables of Luke 15?
adults.option_a: The Pharisees and scribes murmured because Jesus received sinners and ate with them — the three parables are Jesus' defence of His ministry to the lost, revealing God's heart for the outcast
adults.option_b: Jesus was teaching in the temple
adults.option_c: The disciples asked for a parable
adults.option_d: A crowd demanded entertainment
adults.explanation: Luke 15:1-2 — "Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him. And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them." All three parables respond to this criticism.

adults.question: How do the three parables (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son) form a literary progression?
adults.option_a: They escalate in value and intimacy: from one of a hundred sheep to one of ten coins to one of two sons — the ratio of lost to found increases, the searching becomes more personal, and the final parable adds the dimension of free will (the son chose to leave)
adults.option_b: They all tell the same story
adults.option_c: Each parable contradicts the previous one
adults.option_d: They decrease in importance
adults.explanation: Luke 15:4,8,11 — The progression moves from animal (sheep) to object (coin) to person (son), from 1/100 to 1/10 to 1/2, and from wandering (sheep) to being lost passively (coin) to deliberate departure (son).

adults.question: What does the father running to meet the prodigal son reveal about God?
adults.option_a: In ancient Near Eastern culture, a dignified patriarch would never run — the father's running displays undignified, extravagant grace that absorbs the shame the son deserved. It pictures God's eagerness to restore repentant sinners
adults.option_b: The father was exercising for health
adults.option_c: Running was normal for wealthy men
adults.option_d: The father was angry and rushing to punish
adults.explanation: Luke 15:20 — "When he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran." Kenneth Bailey notes that a Middle Eastern patriarch running would hike up his robes, exposing his legs — a culturally humiliating act done out of love.

adults.question: What do the robe, ring, and shoes symbolise in the prodigal's restoration?
adults.option_a: The best robe signifies honour and full sonship; the ring represents authority and family identity; shoes distinguish a son from a slave (who went barefoot) — full restoration, not servanthood, is the father's gift
adults.option_b: They were simply practical gifts
adults.option_c: They meant the son would become a servant
adults.option_d: They were given reluctantly
adults.explanation: Luke 15:22 — "The best robe" (honour), "ring" (signet ring = authority), "shoes" (free son, not barefoot slave). The father does not even let the son finish his rehearsed speech before restoring him completely.

adults.question: Who does the elder brother represent in the parable, and what is his problem?
adults.option_a: He represents the Pharisees and scribes — those who serve God dutifully but resent His grace toward sinners. His problem is a works-based mentality that sees obedience as earning merit rather than flowing from love
adults.option_b: He represents the Roman government
adults.option_c: He represents the Gentile nations
adults.option_d: He represents the prodigal's friends
adults.explanation: Luke 15:29 — "Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment." The elder brother's language reveals a servant mentality, not a son's heart. He never says "my brother" but "thy son" (v.30), distancing himself.

adults.question: What does the father's response "Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine" correct about the elder brother's complaint?
adults.option_a: The elder brother already had everything — his complaint reveals he never appreciated what he had. He served out of duty, not love, and failed to understand that the father's generosity to the prodigal did not diminish his own inheritance
adults.option_b: The father was dismissing the elder brother
adults.option_c: The elder brother lost his inheritance
adults.option_d: The father chose the prodigal over the elder
adults.explanation: Luke 15:31 — "Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine." The father gently corrects: the elder son's inheritance was never threatened. His resentment reveals he viewed his relationship as transactional rather than filial.

adults.question: What does the prodigal's statement "I have sinned against heaven, and before thee" reveal about the nature of sin?
adults.option_a: All sin is ultimately vertical (against God/"heaven") before it is horizontal (against others) — echoing David's confession in Psalm 51:4, "Against thee, thee only, have I sinned." The prodigal recognises that his rebellion was first and foremost against God
adults.option_b: Sin only affects other people
adults.option_c: Sin is a private matter with no consequences
adults.option_d: Heaven has nothing to do with sin
adults.explanation: Luke 15:18 — "I have sinned against heaven, and before thee." The order is significant: heaven first, then the earthly father. This shows genuine repentance recognises the God-ward dimension of all sin (cf. Psalm 51:4).

adults.question: Why does the parable leave the elder brother's response unresolved?
adults.option_a: The open ending is deliberate — Jesus leaves the Pharisees (whom the elder brother represents) to decide whether they will join the feast or remain outside in resentful self-righteousness. It is an invitation, not a condemnation
adults.option_b: Luke forgot to finish the parable
adults.option_c: The elder brother's response is obvious
adults.option_d: The parable was actually complete
adults.explanation: Luke 15:32 — The father pleads with the elder son but we never learn if he enters. This open ending mirrors Jesus' real-time appeal to the Pharisees: Will you join the celebration of God's grace toward sinners, or will you remain outside?

kids.question: What did Jesus say about serving two masters?
kids.option_a: No servant can serve two masters — you cannot serve God and money
kids.option_b: You can serve as many masters as you want
kids.option_c: Money is the most important thing
kids.option_d: God wants you to focus on getting rich
kids.explanation: Luke 16:13 — "No servant can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon."

kids.question: In the story of the rich man and Lazarus, who lay at the rich man's gate?
kids.option_a: A poor beggar named Lazarus who was full of sores
kids.explanation: Luke 16:20 — "And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores."

kids.question: What happened to Lazarus when he died?
kids.option_a: He was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom — a place of comfort and rest
kids.option_b: He went to a place of suffering
kids.explanation: Luke 16:22 — "And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom."

teens.question: What lesson did Jesus draw from the parable of the unjust steward?
teens.option_a: He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much — use worldly resources wisely to make friends for eternity
teens.option_b: Cheating is always acceptable
teens.option_c: Dishonesty is a virtue
teens.option_d: Money does not matter at all
teens.explanation: Luke 16:10 — "He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about what men highly esteem?
teens.option_a: That which is highly esteemed amongst men is abomination in the sight of God
teens.option_b: Whatever men value is also valued by God
teens.option_c: God and men always agree
teens.option_d: Human standards are always correct
teens.explanation: Luke 16:15 — "Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed amongst men is abomination in the sight of God."

teens.question: What did the rich man in torment ask Abraham to do?
teens.option_a: Send Lazarus to dip his finger in water and cool his tongue, for he was tormented in the flame
teens.option_b: Let him return to earth as a rich man
teens.option_c: Give him a second chance at life
teens.option_d: Send food from Abraham's table
teens.explanation: Luke 16:24 — "And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame."

teens.question: What reason did Abraham give for not sending Lazarus to warn the rich man's brothers?
teens.option_a: They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them — if they will not believe Scripture, they will not be persuaded even if one rose from the dead
teens.option_b: Abraham said he would send Lazarus
teens.option_c: Abraham had no way to communicate with earth
teens.option_d: The brothers already knew the truth
teens.explanation: Luke 16:29,31 — "They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them... If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead."

teens.question: What did Abraham say about the great gulf between heaven and hell?
teens.option_a: There is a great gulf fixed between the place of comfort and the place of torment, so that none can cross from one side to the other
teens.option_b: People can freely cross back and forth
teens.option_c: The gulf will eventually disappear
teens.option_d: Only angels can cross the gulf
teens.explanation: Luke 16:26 — "And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot; neither can they pass to us, that would come from thence."

adults.question: What is Jesus commending in the parable of the unjust steward — dishonesty or shrewdness?
adults.option_a: The lord commended the steward's shrewdness (prudent use of present resources for future security), not his dishonesty — Jesus says the children of this world are wiser in their generation than the children of light. Believers should be equally strategic with eternal investments
adults.option_b: Jesus is endorsing dishonesty
adults.option_c: Stealing is acceptable for Christians
adults.option_d: The parable has no moral lesson
adults.explanation: Luke 16:8 — "The lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light." The commendation is for foresight and decisive action, not for dishonesty.

adults.question: What does "make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness" mean?
adults.option_a: Use worldly wealth strategically for eternal purposes — invest in people and God's kingdom so that when earthly resources fail, those blessed by your generosity will welcome you into everlasting habitations
adults.option_b: Become friends with wealthy sinners
adults.option_c: Money is evil and should be destroyed
adults.option_d: Bribery is acceptable in God's kingdom
adults.explanation: Luke 16:9 — "Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations." The paradox is that unrighteous mammon can serve righteous ends through wise, generous stewardship.

adults.question: Is the Rich Man and Lazarus a parable or a historical account, and what is its primary teaching?
adults.option_a: Whether parable or historical account, its primary teaching is that eternal destinies are fixed at death, earthly wealth provides no advantage in the afterlife, and Scripture (Moses and the prophets) is sufficient for salvation — miracles will not convince those who reject God's word
adults.option_b: It is purely fictional with no lessons
adults.option_c: It teaches that all rich people go to hell
adults.option_d: It only applies to first-century Jews
adults.explanation: Luke 16:19-31 — The account is unique in using a proper name (Lazarus). Its teachings — reversal of earthly fortunes, the finality of death, the sufficiency of Scripture — are its theological core regardless of genre classification.

adults.question: What does the "great gulf fixed" between Abraham's bosom and Hades teach about the afterlife?
adults.option_a: After death, destinies are permanent and irreversible — there is no crossing from torment to comfort or vice versa, ruling out post-mortem repentance, soul sleep, or second chances
adults.option_b: The gulf is temporary and will dissolve
adults.option_c: People can pray their way across
adults.option_d: The afterlife is the same for everyone
adults.explanation: Luke 16:26 — "Between us and you there is a great gulf fixed." The permanence of the gulf teaches the irreversibility of eternal destinies, urging repentance in this life as the only window of opportunity.

adults.question: What prophetic irony exists in Abraham's statement "neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead"?
adults.option_a: This anticipates Jesus' own resurrection — even after Christ rose from the dead, many who rejected Scripture still refused to believe, proving Abraham's point that miracles cannot overcome wilful rejection of God's word
adults.option_b: Abraham was wrong about this prediction
adults.option_c: Everyone believed after Jesus rose
adults.option_d: The resurrection convinced all the Pharisees
adults.explanation: Luke 16:31 — The dramatic irony is overwhelming: Jesus (a man named in connection with Lazarus imagery) will rise from the dead, yet the religious leaders will still not believe. Scripture rejected cannot be replaced by miracles.

adults.question: What does "the law and the prophets were until John" mean about salvation history?
adults.option_a: The era of the Law and Prophets culminated in John the Baptist, and since then the kingdom of God is preached — marking a turning point in redemptive history from the old covenant era to the new kingdom proclamation
adults.option_b: The Old Testament is now irrelevant
adults.option_c: John the Baptist ended all prophecy
adults.option_d: The law was abolished completely
adults.explanation: Luke 16:16 — "The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it." This does not abolish the law (v.17) but marks a new epoch where the kingdom is actively offered.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail"?
adults.option_a: The law's authority is permanent and inviolable — a "tittle" (keraia) is the smallest stroke distinguishing Hebrew letters, meaning even the most minute detail of God's word will be fulfilled
adults.option_b: The law is easily changed
adults.option_c: Only major commandments matter
adults.option_d: The law failed long ago
adults.explanation: Luke 16:17 — "And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail." This affirms the enduring authority of Scripture even as the kingdom era dawns, preventing antinomian conclusions from verse 16.

adults.question: What does the reversal of fortunes between the rich man and Lazarus teach about wealth and poverty?
adults.option_a: Wealth without compassion leads to judgment while suffering endured in faith leads to comfort — Abraham says "thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented." The reversal reflects Luke's consistent theme of eschatological reversal
adults.option_b: Poverty automatically guarantees heaven
adults.option_c: Wealth automatically leads to hell
adults.option_d: Economic status has no spiritual significance
adults.explanation: Luke 16:25 — The reversal is not mechanical (rich=damned, poor=saved) but the rich man's callous indifference to Lazarus at his gate (sins of omission) while having Moses and the prophets (v.29) reveals culpable disregard for both God's word and God's image-bearer.

kids.question: How many lepers did Jesus heal at once?
kids.option_a: Ten lepers
kids.explanation: Luke 17:12 — "And as he entered into a certain village, there met him ten men that were lepers, which stood afar off."

kids.question: How many of the ten healed lepers came back to say thank you to Jesus?
kids.option_a: Only one — and he was a Samaritan
kids.explanation: Luke 17:15-16 — "And one of them, when he saw that he was healed, turned back, and with a loud voice glorified God, And fell down on his face at his feet, giving him thanks: and he was a Samaritan."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about faith as small as a mustard seed?
kids.option_a: Even faith that small could tell a tree to be pulled up and planted in the sea, and it would obey
kids.option_b: Mustard seed faith is too small to do anything
kids.option_c: You need faith as big as a mountain
kids.option_d: Only grown-ups can have real faith
kids.explanation: Luke 17:6 — "If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto this sycamine tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about those who cause little ones to stumble?
teens.option_a: It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than that he should offend one of these little ones
teens.option_b: Causing others to stumble is not a big deal
teens.option_c: Only teachers can cause stumbling
teens.option_d: God overlooks those who lead others astray
teens.explanation: Luke 17:2 — "It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than that he should offend one of these little ones."

teens.question: What question did Jesus ask about the nine lepers who did not return?
teens.option_a: Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine? There are not found that returned to give glory to God, save this stranger
teens.option_b: Jesus did not notice they were gone
teens.option_c: Jesus said it was fine they did not return
teens.option_d: Jesus sent the disciples to find them
teens.explanation: Luke 17:17-18 — "And Jesus answering said, Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine? There are not found that returned to give glory to God, save this stranger."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the location of the kingdom of God?
teens.option_a: The kingdom of God is within you — it does not come with outward observation
teens.option_b: The kingdom of God is in Jerusalem only
teens.option_c: The kingdom of God is in a faraway place
teens.option_d: The kingdom of God is visible to everyone
teens.explanation: Luke 17:20-21 — "The kingdom of God cometh not with observation: Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you."

teens.question: What Old Testament examples did Jesus use to describe the days of the Son of man?
teens.option_a: The days of Moses and the Red Sea
teens.option_b: The days of Noah (people eating and drinking until the flood came) and the days of Lot (people eating and drinking until fire fell on Sodom) — sudden destruction came upon the unprepared
teens.option_c: The days of David and Solomon
teens.option_d: The days of Elijah and Elisha
teens.explanation: Luke 17:26-29 — "And as it was in the days of Noe, so shall it be also in the days of the Son of man... Likewise also as it was in the days of Lot... Even thus shall it be in the day when the Son of man is revealed."

teens.question: What warning did Jesus give using the example of Lot's wife?
teens.option_a: Remember Lot's wife — she looked back and was turned into a pillar of salt. Do not look back or cling to worldly things when the day of judgment comes
teens.option_b: Lot's wife was an example of faith
teens.option_c: Looking back is perfectly safe
teens.option_d: Lot's wife was saved in the end
teens.explanation: Luke 17:32 — "Remember Lot's wife." The brief command recalls Genesis 19:26. She looked back (longing for what she left behind), becoming a warning against attachment to the world when God calls for decisive departure.

adults.question: What does the account of the ten lepers and only one returning reveal about gratitude and faith?
adults.option_a: Healing was given to all ten, but only the Samaritan (a social and religious outsider) returned to give glory to God — Jesus links his gratitude to saving faith: "thy faith hath made thee whole." The nine received physical healing but missed the deeper spiritual blessing
adults.option_b: All ten lepers were equally grateful
